class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="introduction"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Introduction</h2></div>
<p>Finding the perfect job can feel like searching for a needle in a haystack. As a developer, you might spend hours scanning through job boards, trying to determine if each position matches your skills and experience. What if we could automate this process using AI?</p>
<p>In this tutorial, we&#x27;ll build a sophisticated job matching system that combines several powerful technologies:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Firecrawl</strong> for intelligent web scraping of job postings and resume parsing</li>
<li><strong>Claude 3.5 Sonnet</strong> for job matching analysis</li>
<li><strong>Supabase</strong> for managing job sources and tracking</li>
<li><strong>Discord</strong> for when there is a matching job</li>
<li><strong>Streamlit</strong> for a user-friendly web interface</li>
</ul>
<p>Our application will:</p>
<ol>
<li>Automatically scrape user-provided job boards at regular intervals</li>
<li>Parse your resume from a PDF</li>
<li>Use AI to evaluate each job posting against your qualifications</li>
<li>Send notifications to Discord when strong matches are found</li>
<li>Provide a web interface for managing job sources and viewing results</li>
</ol>
<p><img src="/images/blog/resume_parser/demo.png" alt="Screenshot of the AI resume job matching app showing the main interface with job sources sidebar and resume upload section"/></p>
<p>By the end of this tutorial, you&#x27;ll have a fully automated job search assistant that runs in the cloud and helps you focus on the opportunities that matter most. Whether you&#x27;re actively job hunting or just keeping an eye on the market, this tool will save you countless hours of manual searching and evaluation.</p>
<p>If this project sounds interesting, you can start using it straight away by cloning <a class="!underline" href="https://github.com/BexTuychiev/ai-resume-job-matching">its GitHub repository</a>. The local setup instructions are provided in the README.</p>
<p>On the other hand, if you want to understand how the different parts of the project work together, continue reading!</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="overview-of-the-app"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Overview of the App</h2></div>
<p>Before diving into the technical details, let&#x27;s walk through a typical user journey to understand how the app works.</p>
<p>The process starts with the user adding web pages with job listings. Here are examples of acceptable pages:</p>
<ul>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">https://datacamp.com/jobs</code></li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">https://openai.com/careers/search/</code></li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">https://apply.workable.com/huggingface</code></li>
</ul>
<p>As you can probably tell from the example URLs, the app doesn&#x27;t work with popular job platforms like Indeed or Glassdoor. This is because these platforms already have sophisticated job matching functionality built into their systems. Instead, this app focuses on company career pages and job boards that don&#x27;t offer automated matching - places where you&#x27;d normally have to manually review each posting. This allows you to apply the same intelligent matching to opportunities that might otherwise slip through the cracks.</p>
<p>Each job listings source is added to a Supabase database under the hood for persistence and displayed in the sidebar (you have the option to delete them). After the user inputs all job sources, they can add their PDF link in the main section of the app.</p>
<p>The app uses <a class="!underline" href="https://firecrawl.dev">Firecrawl</a>, an AI-powered scraping engine that extracts structured data from webpages and PDF documents. To parse resumes, Firecrawl requires a direct file link to the PDF.</p>
<p>After parsing the resume, the app crawls all job sources using Firecrawl to gather job listings. Each listing is then analyzed against the resume by Claude to determine compatibility. The UI clearly shows whether a candidate is qualified for each position, along with Claude&#x27;s reasoning. For matching jobs, the app automatically sends notifications to the user&#x27;s Discord account via a webhook.</p>
<p><img src="/images/blog/resume_parser/discord_alert.png" alt="Screenshot of a Discord notification showing a job match alert with details about the position and match analysis"/></p>
<p>The app automatically rechecks all job sources weekly to ensure you never miss a great opportunity.</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="the-tech-stack-used-in-the-app"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">The Tech Stack Used in the App</h2></div>
<p>Building a reliable job matching system requires careful selection of tools that can handle complex tasks while remaining maintainable and cost-effective. Let&#x27;s explore the core technologies that power our application and why each was chosen:</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="1.-,[object-object],-for-ai-powered-web-scraping"></span><h3>1. <a class="!underline" href="https://firecrawl.ai">Firecrawl</a> for AI-powered web scraping</h3></div>
<p>At the heart of our job discovery system is Firecrawl, an AI-powered web scraping engine. Unlike traditional scraping libraries that rely on brittle HTML selectors, Firecrawl uses natural language understanding to identify and extract content. This makes it ideal for our use case because:</p>
<ul>
<li>It can handle diverse job board layouts without custom code for each site</li>
<li>Maintains reliability even when websites update their structure</li>
<li>Automatically bypasses common anti-bot measures</li>
<li>Handles JavaScript-rendered content out of the box</li>
<li>Provides clean, structured data through <a class="!underline" href="https://pydantic.dev">Pydantic</a> schemas</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="2.-,[object-object],-for-job-matching"></span><h3>2. <a class="!underline" href="https://www.anthropic.com/claude">Claude 3.5 Sonnet</a> for job matching</h3></div>
<p>For the critical task of evaluating job fit, we use Claude 3.5 Sonnet through the <a class="!underline" href="https://www.langchain.com">LangChain</a> framework. This AI model excels at understanding both job requirements and candidate qualifications in context. We chose Claude because:</p>
<ul>
<li>Handles complex job requirements effectively</li>
<li>Offers consistent and reliable evaluations</li>
<li>More cost-effective than GPT-4 for this use case</li>
<li>Integrates seamlessly with LangChain for structured outputs</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="3.-,[object-object],-for-data-management"></span><h3>3. <a class="!underline" href="https://supabase.com">Supabase</a> for data management</h3></div>
<p>To manage job sources and tracking, we use Supabase as our database backend. This modern database platform offers:</p>
<ul>
<li>PostgreSQL database with a generous free tier</li>
<li>Real-time capabilities for future features</li>
<li>Simple REST API for database operations</li>
<li>Built-in authentication system</li>
<li>Excellent developer experience with their Python SDK</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="4.-,[object-object],-for-notifications"></span><h3>4. <a class="!underline" href="https://discord.com">Discord</a> for Notifications</h3></div>
<p>When a matching job is found, our system sends notifications through Discord webhooks. This might seem like an unusual choice, but Discord offers several advantages:</p>
<ul>
<li>Free and widely adopted</li>
<li>Rich message formatting with embeds</li>
<li>Simple webhook integration</li>
<li>Mobile notifications</li>
<li>Supports dedicated channels for job alerts</li>
<li>Threading for discussions about specific opportunities</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="5.-,[object-object],-for-user-interface"></span><h3>5. <a class="!underline" href="https://streamlit.io">Streamlit</a> for user interface</h3></div>
<p>The web interface is built with Streamlit, a Python framework for data applications. We chose Streamlit because:</p>
<ul>
<li>It enables rapid development of data-focused interfaces</li>
<li>Provides built-in components for common UI patterns</li>
<li>Handles async operations smoothly</li>
<li>Offers automatic hot-reloading during development</li>
<li>Requires no JavaScript knowledge</li>
<li>Makes deployment straightforward</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="6.-,[object-object],-for-automation"></span><h3>6. <a class="!underline" href="https://github.com/features/actions">GitHub Actions</a> for automation</h3></div>
<p>To ensure regular job checking, we use GitHub Actions for scheduling. This service provides:</p>
<ul>
<li>Free scheduling for public repositories</li>
<li>Built-in secret management</li>
<li>Reliable cron scheduling</li>
<li>Easy maintenance and modifications</li>
<li>Integrated version control</li>
<li>Comprehensive logging and monitoring</li>
</ul>
<p>This carefully selected stack provides a robust foundation while keeping costs minimal through generous free tiers. The combination of AI-powered tools (Firecrawl and Claude) with modern infrastructure (Supabase, Discord, GitHub Actions) creates a reliable and scalable job matching system that can grow with your needs.</p>
<p>Most importantly, this stack minimizes maintenance overhead - a crucial factor for any automated system. The AI-powered components adapt to changes automatically, while the infrastructure services are managed by their respective providers, letting you focus on finding your next great opportunity rather than maintaining the system.</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="breaking-down-the-app-components"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Breaking Down the App Components</h2></div>
<p>When you look at <a class="!underline" href="https://github.com/BexTuychiev/ai-resume-job-matching">the GitHub repository</a> of the app, you will see the following file structure:</p>
<p><img src="/images/blog/resume_parser/github_snapshot.png" alt="GitHub repository file structure showing key files like models.py, scraper.py, and app.py"/></p>
<p>Several files in the repository serve common purposes that most developers will recognize:</p>
<ul>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">.gitignore</code>: Specifies which files Git should ignore when tracking changes</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">README.md</code>: Documentation explaining what the project does and how to use it</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">requirements.txt</code>: Lists all Python package dependencies needed to run the project</li>
</ul>
<p>Let&#x27;s examine the remaining Python scripts and understand how they work together to power the application. The explanations will be in a logical order building from foundational elements to higher-level functionality.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="1.-core-data-structures---,[object-object]"></span><h3>1. Core data structures - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/models.py</code></h3></div>
<p>At the heart of our job matching system are three Pydantic models that define the core data structures used throughout the application. These models not only provide type safety and validation but also serve as schemas that guide Firecrawl&#x27;s AI in extracting structured data from web pages.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 fill="var(--border-faint)"></path></svg><div class="p-32 bg-black/1 relative overflow-x-auto overflow-y-auto max-h-400"><pre tabindex="0" data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">class</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Job</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">BaseModel</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    title</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> str</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Job title&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> str</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;URL of the job posting&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    company</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> str</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Company name&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">Job</code> model represents an individual job posting with three essential fields:</p>
<ul>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">title</code>: The position&#x27;s name</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">url</code>: Direct link to the job posting</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">company</code>: Name of the hiring organization</li>
</ul>
<p>This model is used by both the scraper to extract job listings and the Discord notifier to format job match notifications. The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">Field</code> descriptions guide the Firecrawl&#x27;s AI to better locate the HTML/CSS components containing the relevant information.</p>

style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">BaseModel</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> str</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;URL of the job board&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    last_checked</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> Optional</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">[</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">datetime</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">]</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Last check timestamp&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobSource</code> model tracks job board URLs and when they were last checked:</p>
<ul>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">url</code>: The job board&#x27;s web address</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">last_checked</code>: Optional timestamp of the last scraping attempt</li>
</ul>
<p>This model is primarily used by the database component to manage job sources and the scheduler to track when sources need to be rechecked.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">class</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> JobListings</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">BaseModel</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    jobs</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> List</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">[</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">Job</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">]</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> =</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Field</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;List of job postings&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Finally, the <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobListings</code> model serves as a container for multiple <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">Job</code> objects. This model is crucial for the scraper component, as it tells Firecrawl to extract all job postings from a page rather than just the first one it finds.</p>
<p>These models form the foundation of our application&#x27;s data flow:</p>
<ol>
<li>The scraper uses them to extract structured data from web pages</li>
<li>The database uses them to store and retrieve job sources</li>
<li>The matcher uses them to process job details</li>
<li>The Discord notifier uses them to format notifications</li>
</ol>
<p>By defining these data structures upfront, we ensure consistency throughout the application and make it easier to modify the data model in the future if needed.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="2.-database-operations---,[object-object]"></span><h3>2. Database operations - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/database.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The database component handles persistence of job sources using Supabase, a PostgreSQL-based backend service. This module provides essential CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations for managing job board URLs and their check history.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 fill="var(--border-faint)"></path></svg><div class="p-32 bg-black/1 relative overflow-x-auto overflow-y-auto max-h-400"><pre tabindex="0" data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">class</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> Database</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> __init__</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)">self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        url </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> os</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">getenv</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;SUPABASE_URL&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        key </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> os</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">getenv</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;SUPABASE_KEY&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">client </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> create_client</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(url, key)</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> save_job_source</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)">self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)"> url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> str</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">) </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">-&gt;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> None</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">        &quot;&quot;&quot;Save a job source to the database&quot;&quot;&quot;</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">client</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">table</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;job_sources&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">).</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">upsert</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            {</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;url&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">: url, </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;last_checked&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">: </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">None</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">        ).</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">execute</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">()</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">Database</code> class initializes a connection to Supabase using environment variables and provides four key methods:</p>
<ol>
<li>
<p><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">save_job_source</code>: Adds or updates a job board URL in the database. The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">upsert</code> operation ensures no duplicate entries are created.</p>
</li>
<li>
<p><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">delete_job_source</code>: Removes a job source from tracking:</p>
</li>
</ol>

<p>This database component is used by:</p>
<ul>
<li>The Streamlit interface (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">app.py</code>) for managing job sources through the sidebar</li>
<li>The scheduler (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">scheduler.py</code>) for tracking when sources were last checked</li>
<li>The automated GitHub Action workflow for persistent storage between runs</li>
</ul>
<p>By using Supabase, we get a reliable, scalable database with minimal setup and maintenance requirements. The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobSource</code> model we defined earlier ensures type safety when working with the database records throughout the application.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="3.-scraping-with-firecrawl---,[object-object]"></span><h3>3. Scraping with Firecrawl - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/scraper.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The scraper component handles all web scraping operations using Firecrawl, an AI-powered scraping engine. This module is responsible for parsing resumes and extracting job listings from various sources.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 <p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobScraper</code> class initializes a Firecrawl connection and provides three main methods:</p>
<ol>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">parse_resume</code>: Extracts text content from a PDF resume. Uses Streamlit&#x27;s caching to avoid re-parsing the same resume:</li>
</ol>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 <ol start="2">
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">scrape_job_postings</code>: Batch scrapes multiple job board URLs using the <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobListings</code> schema to guide Firecrawl&#x27;s extraction:</li>
</ol>

style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">:</span></span>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            urls</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">source_urls,</span></span>

<p>If you want to understand Firecrawl&#x27;s syntax better, refer to our <a class="!underline" href="https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint">separate guide on its <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">/scrape</code> endpoint</a>.</p>
<ol start="3">
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">scrape_job_content</code>: Retrieves the full content of a specific job posting for detailed analysis:</li>
</ol>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        return</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> response</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">[</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;markdown&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">]</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>This entire scraper component is used by:</p>
<ul>
<li>The Streamlit interface (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">app.py</code>) for initial resume parsing and job discovery</li>
<li>The scheduler (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">scheduler.py</code>) for automated periodic job checks</li>
<li>The matcher component for detailed job content analysis</li>
</ul>
<p>The use of Firecrawl&#x27;s AI capabilities allows the scraper to handle diverse webpage layouts without custom selectors, while Streamlit&#x27;s caching helps optimize performance by avoiding redundant resume parsing.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="4.-job-matching-with-claude---,[object-object]"></span><h3>4. Job matching with Claude - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/matcher.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The matcher component uses Claude 3.5 Sonnet through LangChain to evaluate whether a candidate&#x27;s resume matches a job posting. This module provides intelligent job fit analysis with structured outputs.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 <span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;is_match&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Whether the candidate is a good fit for the job (true/false)&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            ),</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">            ResponseSchema</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;reason&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Brief explanation of why the candidate is or isn&#x27;t a good fit&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            ),</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        ]</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobMatcher</code> class initializes with two key components:</p>
<ol>
<li>
<p>A Claude instance configured for consistent outputs (temperature=0)</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>Response schemas that define the structure of the matching results:</p>
<ul>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">is_match</code>: Boolean indicating if the candidate is qualified</li>
<li><code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">reason</code>: Explanation of the matching decision</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">    ])</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">output_parser </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> StructuredOutputParser</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">from_response_schemas</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">        self.response_schemas</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">    )</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<blockquote>
<p>Note: The system prompt significantly affects how jobs are matched. You can make it more relaxed or strict when evaluating candidates. Use a looser prompt if you want to apply to more jobs, or a stricter one if you&#x27;re being more selective.</p>
</blockquote>
<p>The class also sets up:</p>
<ul>
<li>A prompt template that positions Claude as an expert interviewer and sets a high bar for matches (75% of requirements)</li>
<li>An output parser that ensures responses follow the defined schema</li>
</ul>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">        )</span></span>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        return</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">output_parser</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">parse</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(response.content)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">evaluate_match</code> method:</p>
<ol>
<li>Takes a resume and job posting as input</li>
<li>Formats the prompt with the provided content</li>
<li>Sends the request to Claude</li>
<li>Parses and returns the structured response</li>
</ol>
<p>This entire matcher component is used by:</p>
<ul>
<li>The Streamlit interface (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">app.py</code>) for real-time job matching</li>
<li>The scheduler (<code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">scheduler.py</code>) for automated matching checks</li>
<li>The Discord notifier to determine when to send alerts</li>
</ul>
<p>By using Claude with structured outputs, we ensure consistent and reliable job matching that can be easily integrated into the broader application workflow.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="5.-sending-notifications-with-discord---,[object-object]"></span><h3>5. Sending notifications with Discord - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/discord.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The Discord component handles sending notifications when matching jobs are found. It uses Discord&#x27;s webhook functionality to deliver rich, formatted messages about job matches.</p>

<p>First, we initialize the notifier with a Discord webhook URL from environment variables. This URL is where all notifications will be sent.</p>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            title</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;🎯 New Job Match Found!&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            description</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;**</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">job.title</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">** at **</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">job.company</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">**</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">\n\n</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">match_reason</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            color</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;5865F2&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,  </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)"># Discord&#x27;s blue color scheme</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">        )</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">send_match</code> method creates the notification:</p>
<ul>
<li>Takes a <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">Job</code> object and the AI&#x27;s matching reason as input</li>
<li>Creates a webhook connection to Discord</li>
<li>Builds an embed message with:<!-- -->
<ul>
<li>An eye-catching title with emoji</li>
<li>Job title and company in bold</li>
<li>The AI&#x27;s explanation of why this job matches</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;🔗 Job URL&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">, value</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;[Apply Here](</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">job.url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">)&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">, inline</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">True</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">        )</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        webhook</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">add_embed</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(embed)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        webhook</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">execute</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">()</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Finally, the method:</p>
<ul>
<li>Adds structured fields for company and job URL</li>
<li>Uses emojis for visual appeal</li>
<li>Creates a clickable &quot;Apply Here&quot; link</li>
<li>Sends the formatted message to Discord</li>
</ul>
<p>This component is used by:</p>
<ul>
<li>The matcher component when a job match is found</li>
<li>The scheduler for automated notifications</li>
<li>The Streamlit interface for real-time match alerts</li>
</ul>
<p>The use of Discord embeds provides a clean, professional look for notifications while making it easy for users to access job details and apply links directly from the message.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="6.-automated-source-checking-script---,[object-object]"></span><h3>6. Automated source checking script - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">src/scheduler.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The scheduler component handles automated periodic checking of job sources, coordinating between all other components to continuously monitor for new matching positions.</p>

<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">JobScheduler</code> class initializes with:</p>
<ul>
<li>All necessary components (scraper, matcher, notifier, database)</li>
<li>Resume URL from environment variables</li>
<li>Configurable check interval (defaults to 15 minutes)</li>
<li>A set to track processed jobs and avoid duplicates</li>
<li>Logging setup for monitoring operations</li>
</ul>

<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">process_source</code> method starts by:</p>
<ul>
<li>Logging the current operation</li>
<li>Parsing the user&#x27;s resume</li>
<li>Scraping all jobs from the given source</li>
</ul>

<p>For each job found, it:</p>
<ul>
<li>Skips if already processed</li>
<li>Scrapes the full job description</li>
<li>Evaluates the match against the resume</li>
<li>Sends a Discord notification if it&#x27;s a match</li>
<li>Marks the job as processed</li>
</ul>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">                logger</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">info</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Found </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">len</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(sources)</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> job sources&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">run</code> method starts the main loop by:</p>
<ul>
<li>Getting all job sources from the database</li>
<li>Logging the number of sources found</li>
</ul>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">                            f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(source.last_checked </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">+</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> timedelta</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(minutes</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">self.check_interval) </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">-</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)"> datetime.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">utcnow</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">()).</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">total_seconds</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">() </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">/</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> 60</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:.1f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> minutes&quot;</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                        )</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">                await</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> asyncio</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">sleep</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">60</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">  # Check every minute</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>For each source, it:</p>
<ul>
<li>Checks if it needs processing (never checked or interval elapsed)</li>
<li>Processes the source if needed</li>
<li>Logs skipped sources with time until next check</li>
<li>Waits a minute before the next iteration</li>
</ul>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">                await</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> asyncio</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">sleep</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">60</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Error handling:</p>
<ul>
<li>Catches and logs any exceptions</li>
<li>Waits a minute before retrying</li>
<li>Ensures the scheduler keeps running despite errors</li>
</ul>
<p>This component is used by:</p>
<ul>
<li>The GitHub Actions workflow for automated checks</li>
<li>The command-line interface for manual checks</li>
<li>The logging system for monitoring and debugging</li>
</ul>
<p>The extensive logging helps track operations and diagnose issues, while the modular design allows for easy maintenance and updates.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="7.-user-interface-with-streamlit---,[object-object]"></span><h3>7. User interface with Streamlit - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">app.py</code></h3></div>
<p>The Streamlit interface provides a user-friendly way to manage job sources and run manual job matching checks. Let&#x27;s break down each component:</p>
<ol>
<li>First, we set up the necessary imports and helper functions:</li>
</ol>

<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">process_job</code> function handles the core job matching logic for a single posting:</p>
<ol>
<li>
<p>Scrapes the full job content using the provided URL</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>Evaluates if the resume matches the job requirements</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>Sends a notification if there&#x27;s a match</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>Returns both the job and match result for further processing</p>
</li>
<li>
<p>The main application setup and sidebar for managing job sources:</p>
</li>
</ol>

<p>The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">main()</code> function sets up the core Streamlit application interface:</p>
<ol>
<li>Creates a title for the app</li>
<li>Initializes the key services (scraper, matcher, notifier, database)</li>
<li>Adds a sidebar with controls for managing job source URLs</li>
<li>Provides a text input and button to add new job sources</li>
<li>Saves valid sources to the database</li>
</ol>
<p>The sidebar allows users to maintain a list of job boards and company career pages to monitor for new postings.</p>
<ol start="3">
<li>The source management interface:</li>
</ol>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 <p>This section displays the list of current job sources and provides delete functionality:</p>
<ol>
<li>Shows a &quot;Current Sources&quot; subheader</li>
<li>Iterates through all sources from the database</li>
<li>Creates a two-column layout for each source</li>
<li>First column shows the source URL</li>
<li>Second column has a delete button</li>
<li>When delete is clicked, removes the source and refreshes the page</li>
</ol>
<p>The delete functionality helps users maintain their source list by removing outdated or unwanted job boards. The <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">rerun()</code> call ensures the UI updates immediately after deletion.</p>
<ol start="4">
<li>The main content area with instructions and resume input:</li>
</ol>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">                st</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">write</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Reason: </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">result[</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&#x27;reason&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">]</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">                st</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">divider</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">()</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        st</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">success</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Analysis complete! Processed </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">len</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(jobs)</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> jobs.&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>This section creates tasks to analyze multiple jobs simultaneously by comparing them against the user&#x27;s resume. As each analysis completes, it displays the results including job title, URL, match status and reasoning. The parallel approach makes the processing more efficient than analyzing jobs one at a time.</p>
<p>The interface provides:</p>
<ul>
<li>A sidebar for managing job sources</li>
<li>Clear instructions for users</li>
<li>Real-time feedback during processing</li>
<li>Visual indicators for matches (✅) and non-matches (❌)</li>
<li>Detailed explanations for each job evaluation</li>
<li>Parallel processing for better performance</li>
</ul>
<p>This component ties together all the backend services into a user-friendly interface that makes it easy to manage job sources and run manual checks.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="8.-github-actions-workflow---,[object-object]"></span><h3>8. GitHub Actions workflow - <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">.github/workflows/scheduler.yml</code></h3></div>
<p>The GitHub Actions workflow automates the job checking process by running the scheduler at regular intervals. Let&#x27;s break down the configuration:</p>
<ol>
<li>First, we define the workflow name and triggers:</li>
</ol>

style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> Job Matcher Scheduler</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">on</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">  push</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    branches</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> [</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)">main</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">]</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">  schedule</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    - </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">cron</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> &quot;0 0 * * 1&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)"> # Run every Monday at midnight</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>This configuration:</p>
<ul>
<li>Names the workflow &quot;Job Matcher Scheduler&quot;</li>
<li>Triggers on pushes to the main branch (for testing)</li>
<li>Runs automatically every Monday at midnight using cron syntax<!-- -->
<ul>
<li>0: Minute (0)</li>
<li>0: Hour (0 = midnight)</li>
<li>*: Day of month (any)</li>
<li>*: Month (any)</li>
<li>1: Day of week (1 = Monday)</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<ol start="2">
<li>Define the job and its environment:</li>
</ol>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">  check-jobs</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    runs-on</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ubuntu-latest</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    steps</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">      - </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">uses</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> actions/checkout@v2</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">      - </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> Set up Python</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        uses</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> actions/setup-python@v2</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        with</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">          python-version</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> &quot;3.10&quot;</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>This section:</p>
<ul>
<li>Creates a job named &quot;check-jobs&quot;</li>
<li>Uses the latest Ubuntu runner</li>
<li>Checks out the repository code</li>
<li>Sets up Python 3.10</li>
</ul>
<ol start="3">
<li>Install dependencies:</li>
</ol>

class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="yml" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">- </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">name</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> Run job checker</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">  env</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    FIRECRAWL_API_KEY</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.FIRECRAWL_API_KEY }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    ANTHROPIC_API_KEY</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    RESUME_URL</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.RESUME_URL }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    SUPABASE_URL</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.SUPABASE_URL }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    SUPABASE_KEY</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> ${{ secrets.SUPABASE_KEY }}</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    CHECK_INTERVAL_MINUTES</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> 15</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">  run</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">:</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> |</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)">    python -m src.scheduler</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>This final step:</p>
<ul>
<li>Sets up all necessary environment variables from GitHub Secrets</li>
<li>Configures the check interval</li>
<li>Runs the scheduler script</li>
</ul>
<p>The workflow provides:</p>
<ul>
<li>Automated weekly job checks</li>
<li>Secure handling of sensitive credentials</li>
<li>Consistent environment for running checks</li>
<li>Detailed logs of each run</li>
<li>Easy modification of the schedule</li>
</ul>
<p>To use this workflow, you need to:</p>
<ol>
<li>Add all required secrets to your GitHub repository</li>
<li>Ensure your repository is public (for free GitHub Actions minutes)</li>
<li>Verify the workflow is enabled in your Actions tab</li>
</ol>
<p>The weekly schedule helps stay within GitHub&#x27;s free tier limits while still regularly checking for new opportunities.</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="conclusion"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Conclusion</h2></div>
<p>We&#x27;ve built a powerful automated job matching system that combines several modern technologies into a cohesive solution. By integrating Firecrawl for web scraping, Claude AI for intelligent matching, Discord for notifications, GitHub Actions for scheduling, and Supabase for storage, we&#x27;ve created a practical tool that automates the tedious parts of job searching. This allows job seekers to focus their energy on more important tasks like preparing for interviews and improving their skills.</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="next-steps"></span><h3>Next Steps</h3></div>
<p>The modular design of this system opens up many possibilities for future enhancements. You could expand support to additional job boards, implement more sophisticated matching algorithms, or add alternative notification methods like email. Consider building a mobile interface or adding analytics to track your application success rates. The foundation we&#x27;ve built makes it easy to adapt and enhance the system as your needs evolve. Feel free to fork the repository and customize it to match your specific job search requirements.</p></div></div></div><div class="h-42 p-30 border-t border-border-faint relative lg-max:hidden"><svg open source frameworks compared: LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen, CrewAI, Google ADK, Dify, and Mastra\n- LangGraph leads in enterprise adoption (34.5M monthly downloads), Dify leads in GitHub stars (129.8k)\n- Firecrawl's /agent endpoint handles web data collection for any framework\n- 10 best practices from Anthropic, OpenAI, and McKinsey for deploying agents in production\n- Comparison table with stars, downloads, features, and best-fit use cases for each framework\n\n---\n\nThe hype around AI agents is very real and well-deserved. According to [Markets And Markets](https://www.marketsandmarkets.com/Market-Reports/ai-agents-market-15761548.html), the global agent market reached $7.84 billion in 2025 and is projected to hit $52.62 billion by 2030, with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 46.3%. [Gartner predicts](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-08-26-gartner-predicts-40-percent-of-enterprise-apps-will-feature-task-specific-ai-agents-by-2026-up-from-less-than-5-percent-in-2025) that 40% of enterprise applications will feature task-specific AI agents by end of 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025. This rapid growth is fueled by stronger and stronger foundational LLMs that seem to come out every week now.\n\nFor this reason, choosing appropriate frameworks to build AI agents has become essential if developers want to integrate existing infrastructure with language models. In this article, we will examine the most powerful and widely adopted open source frameworks to build agents in 2026, analyzing their technical features, implementation requirements and optimal use cases.\n\n**Enhance Your Agents:** Combine agent frameworks with [RAG systems](/blog/best-open-source-rag-frameworks) for knowledge-augmented responses. Use [web scraping libraries](/blog/best-open-source-web-scraping-libraries) and [browser automation tools](/blog/browser-automation-tools-comparison) for data collection.\n\n**Related Guides:** For converting websites into agents, see our [website to agent tutorial](/blog/website-to-agent-with-firecrawl-openai) and [website to LangGraph agent guide](/blog/website-to-langgraph-agent). For OpenAI-specific implementations, check [OpenAI agent builders and Firecrawl](/blog/openai-agent-builders-and-firecrawl).\n\n## Our evaluation methodology to filter agent frameworks\n\nTo find the best agent frameworks, we used clear metrics and practical requirements. We gathered data from GitHub, PyPI downloads, documentation, and industry articles, looking for frameworks that can handle reasoning tasks, work together as multiple agents, and [use tools effectively](/blog/agent-tools).\n\nWe evaluated each framework based on:\n\n- **GitHub metrics**: Stars, active contributors, and regular updates\n- **Adoption**: Monthly download numbers\n- **Technical features**: Ability to reason, collaborate between agents, and use tools\n- **Documentation**: Clear guides and tutorials for developers\n- **Real-world use**: Proven cases in actual production environments\n- **Industry use**: How they work across different sectors like finance and customer service\n- **Backing organizations**: Support from established companies for ongoing development\n\nThe seven frameworks we selected show real value based on these criteria and provide solid options for developers building AI agents in 2026.\n\n## Firecrawl: The best web data collection agent\n\nBefore we get into the list, Firecrawl deserves its own mention. What started as a web scraping API has grown into a full data collection platform for agents. The [/agent endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) handles the hardest part of agent development: getting structured data from the web without writing scraping scripts. You describe what you want in plain text, optionally pass a Pydantic schema, and the agent searches, navigates, and returns structured results.\n\nFirecrawl's agent comes in two models: `spark-1-mini` (default, 60% cheaper) for straightforward extractions, and `spark-1-pro` for complex multi-domain research where accuracy matters most. Both models work without requiring URLs, though you can provide them to focus the search.\n\nHere's an example that finds YC companies and returns structured data:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel, Field\nfrom typing import List, Optional\n\napp = Firecrawl(api_key=\"fc-YOUR-API-KEY\")\n\nclass Company(BaseModel):\n    name: str = Field(description=\"Company name\")\n    batch: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"YC batch\")\n    description: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"One-line description\")\n\nclass CompanyList(BaseModel):\n    companies: List[Company] = Field(description=\"List of companies\")\n\nresult = app.agent(\n    prompt=\"Find 3 YC W22 companies in the consumer category\",\n    schema=CompanyList,\n    model=\"spark-1-mini\"\n)\n\nprint(result.data)\n```\n\n```\n{\n  \"companies\": [\n    {\"name\": \"beU delivery\", \"batch\": \"W22\", \"description\": \"Food delivery service in Africa\"},\n    {\"name\": \"Olympian Motors\", \"batch\": \"W22\", \"description\": \"Next-generation, modular electric vehicles\"},\n    {\"name\": \"SOTERI SKIN\", \"batch\": \"W22\", \"description\": \"D2C skincare for eczema\"}\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nBeyond the agent endpoint, Firecrawl offers a [Browser Sandbox](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/browser) that gives agents a secure, managed browser environment. Each session runs in an isolated sandbox with Playwright pre-installed, and you can execute Python, JavaScript, or bash commands remotely:\n\n```python\n# Launch a cloud browser session\nsession = app.browser()\nprint(session.cdp_url)  # wss://browser.firecrawl.dev/cdp/...\n\n# Execute code in the sandbox\nresult = app.browser_execute(\n    session.id,\n    code='await page.goto(\"https://news.ycombinator.com\")\\ntitle = await page.title()\\nprint(title)',\n    language=\"python\"\n)\n\nprint(result.result)  # \"Hacker News\"\napp.delete_browser(session.id)\n```\n\nThe standard `scrape`, `crawl`, and `batch_scrape` endpoints handle bulk data collection, while the `map` endpoint generates URL lists from entire sites. All of these can be plugged into any agent framework as custom tools. The [LLMs.txt API](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/alpha/llmstxt) converts websites to clean text for LLMs, and the platform includes a [CLI](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/sdks/cli) for terminal-based workflows.\n\n**Visual Development:** Build agent workflows visually with [LangFlow](/blog/langflow-tutorial-visual-ai-workflows) or automate them with [n8n workflows](/blog/firecrawl-n8n-web-automation).\n\n## Top 7 open source frameworks to build AI agents\n\nNow, without further ado, here are the seven most effective open source frameworks for building AI agents in 2026, ranked by their proven success in real-world applications and developer adoption.\n\n### 1. LangGraph - ⭐️24.8k\n\n![LangGraph framework visualization showing directed graph architecture for building AI agents](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/langgraph.webp)\n\n[LangGraph](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph) is a specialized agent framework within the LangChain ecosystem. Released in 2024, it has over 24,800 GitHub stars and 34.5 million monthly downloads. It focuses on building controllable, stateful agents that maintain context throughout interactions. LangGraph integrates with LangSmith for [monitoring agent performance](/blog/best-llm-observability-tools).\n\nCore capabilities:\n\n- Stateful agent orchestration with streaming support\n- Support for single-agent, multi-agent, hierarchical, and sequential control flows\n- Long-term memory and human-in-the-loop workflows\n- Integration with LangChain products like LangSmith\n\nAround 400 companies now use LangGraph Platform to deploy agents in production, including Cisco, Uber, LinkedIn, BlackRock, and JPMorgan. [Klarna's customer support bot](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/case-studies/klarna) handles two-thirds of all customer inquiries, doing the work of 853 employees and saving the company $60 million. [AppFolio's Copilot Realm-X](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/case-studies/appfolio) improved response accuracy by 2x, while [Elastic](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/case-studies/elastic) uses it for AI-powered threat detection in SecOps tasks. For a step-by-step implementation, see our [LangGraph agent tutorial](/blog/langgraph-startup-validator-tutorial).\n\n### 2. OpenAI Agents SDK - ⭐️19k\n\n![OpenAI Agents SDK interface showing Python code implementation for building AI agents with multi-agent workflows and tracing capabilities](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/sdk.webp)\n\nThe [OpenAI Agents SDK](https://github.com/openai/openai-agents-python) is a lightweight Python framework released in March 2025 with over 19,000 GitHub stars and 10.3 million monthly downloads. It focuses on creating multi-agent workflows with tracing and guardrails. The framework is provider-agnostic and compatible with over 100 LLMs.\n\nMain features:\n\n- Lightweight design for multi-agent workflows\n- Comprehensive tracing and guardrails\n- Provider-agnostic compatibility with 100+ LLMs\n- Low learning curve for Python developers\n\nThe SDK has gained traction due to OpenAI's reputation and the framework's versatility. Its documentation provides clear tutorials and API references. While specific enterprise implementations aren't widely documented yet, the framework is designed for applications in finance, customer service, and software development. For a practical application example, see [Converting Websites into Agents with Firecrawl and OpenAI Agents SDK](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/website-to-agent-with-firecrawl-openai), which demonstrates how to transform web content into interactive knowledge agents.\n\n### 3. AutoGen - ⭐️54.6k\n\n![Microsoft AutoGen framework interface showing multi-agent conversation architecture with collaborative AI agents for complex problem-solving](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/autogen.webp)\n\n[AutoGen](https://github.com/microsoft/autogen) is a multi-agent conversation framework developed by Microsoft Research. Released in September 2023, it has grown to over 54,600 GitHub stars and 856,000 monthly downloads. AutoGen uses an event-driven architecture for complex interactions between AI agents and integrates with various LLMs while maintaining structured conversation flows.\n\nKey features include:\n\n- Multi-agent conversation framework with event-driven architecture\n- Scalable workflows for complex collaborative tasks\n- Extensive documentation with tutorials and migration guides\n- Outperforms single-agent solutions on GAIA benchmarks\n\nThe framework has gained traction in data science and education sectors, with [Novo Nordisk implementing it for data science workflows](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/autogen-enabling-next-generation-large-language-model-applications/). In October 2025, Microsoft merged AutoGen with Semantic Kernel into the unified [Microsoft Agent Framework](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework/overview/agent-framework-overview), with GA targeted for end of Q1 2026. AutoGen itself is now in maintenance mode, receiving only bug fixes and security patches, though existing projects continue to work.\n\n### 4. CrewAI - ⭐️44.3k\n\n![CrewAI framework interface showing collaborative AI agents with role-based architecture for orchestrating multi-agent systems in enterprise applications](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/crewai.webp)\n\n[CrewAI](https://github.com/crewAIInc/crewAI) orchestrates role-playing AI agents for collaborative tasks. Launched in early 2024, it has over 44,300 GitHub stars and 5.2 million monthly downloads. Independent from LangChain, CrewAI offers simpler implementation for developers who want to build multi-agent systems without complex dependencies.\n\nMain features:\n\n- Role-playing agent orchestration with defined responsibilities\n- Independence from LangChain for simpler implementation\n- Minimal code required for agent setup\n- Popular in customer service and marketing sectors\n\nCrewAI's straightforward approach has contributed to its rapid adoption. Streaming tool call events were added in January 2026, addressing the earlier limitation around real-time task performance. For a hands-on walkthrough, see our [CrewAI multi-agent tutorial](/blog/crewai-multi-agent-systems-tutorial).\n\n### 5. Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) - ⭐️17.8k\n\n![Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) interface showing modular framework architecture with hierarchical agent compositions and Google ecosystem integration for enterprise AI solutions](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/adk.webp)\n\nThe [Google Agent Dev Kit (ADK)](https://github.com/google/adk-python) was announced in April 2025 and has grown to 17,800 GitHub stars with 3.3 million monthly downloads. This modular framework integrates with the Google ecosystem, including Gemini and Vertex AI. It supports hierarchical agent compositions and custom tools.\n\nADK features:\n\n- Modular framework with Google ecosystem integration\n- Support for hierarchical agent compositions\n- Custom tool development capabilities\n- Efficient development requiring less than 100 lines of code\n\nGoogle uses the ADK in their [Agentspace platform](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/introducing-google-cloud-agentspace) and for customer engagement solutions. The framework has a moderate to steep learning curve due to Google Cloud integration, but comes with detailed documentation. It's well-suited for customer engagement applications and Google Cloud workflow automation. See our [Google ADK tutorial](/blog/google-adk-multi-agent-tutorial) for a practical implementation guide.\n\n### 6. Dify - ⭐️129.8k\n\n![Dify AI agent platform interface showing low-code visual builder with drag-and-drop components for creating enterprise-grade AI agents without programming experience](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/dify.webp)\n\n[Dify](https://github.com/langgenius/dify) is a low-code platform for creating AI agents with over 129,000 GitHub stars. Its visual interface makes it accessible to non-technical users while still offering capabilities for experienced developers. Dify supports hundreds of LLMs and includes RAG, Function Calling, and ReAct strategies.\n\nKey capabilities:\n\n- Low-code visual interface for agent development\n- Built-in RAG, Function Calling, and ReAct strategies\n- Support for hundreds of different LLMs\n- TiDB's Serverless [vector search](/blog/best-vector-databases) for scalability\n\nDify is used across various sectors, from enterprises implementing LLM gateways to startups creating rapid prototypes. Its document generation and financial report analysis features make it valuable in business contexts.\n\n### 7. Mastra - ⭐️21.2k\n\n![Mastra framework interface showing TypeScript-first agent development with graph-based workflows and multi-agent routing](/images/blog/agent-frameworks/mastra.webp)\n\n[Mastra](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra) is a TypeScript-first agent framework built by the team behind Gatsby (the React static site generator). It launched in August 2024 and hit version 1.0 in January 2026, pulling in over 1.77 million monthly NPM downloads since then. The framework is backed by Y Combinator and a $13M seed round from investors including Paul Graham and Guillermo Rauch. Where most agent frameworks on this list target Python developers, Mastra fills the gap for JavaScript and TypeScript teams who want to build agents without switching ecosystems.\n\nMain features:\n\n- Workflows run as graphs with `.then()`, `.branch()`, and `.parallel()` primitives, and they can suspend/resume for human-in-the-loop patterns\n- Any agent can become a routing agent through the `.network()` method, delegating tasks to sub-agents and tools\n- The memory system has four tiers: message history, working memory, semantic recall, and RAG\n- Works with 81 LLM providers and 2,436+ models through the Vercel AI SDK\n- Ships with a local dev playground in the browser for testing agents, visualizing workflow graphs, and running evaluations\n\nMastra has picked up real production traction. Replit uses it in Agent 3 (their AI coding assistant that spins up isolated Docker sandboxes to write and test code), where it improved task success rates from 80% to 96% across thousands of daily sessions. Marsh McLennan deployed a Mastra-based search tool to 75,000 employees, and SoftBank built their Satto Workspace platform on it. The main trade-off is that it's TypeScript-only, so Python-heavy ML teams won't be able to adopt it. Its integration ecosystem is also still growing compared to more established frameworks like LangGraph.\n\n### Comparing all frameworks in a single table\n\n| Framework | Stars | Monthly Downloads | Key Features | Notable Use Cases | Best For |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| LangGraph | 24.8k | 34.5M | • Stateful agent orchestration\u003cbr/\u003e• Multi-agent support\u003cbr/\u003e• Human-in-the-loop workflows | • Klarna: 853 employee-equivalents, $60M saved\u003cbr/\u003e• AppFolio: 2x response accuracy\u003cbr/\u003e• Uber, Cisco, LinkedIn, BlackRock | Enterprise applications requiring state management |\n| OpenAI Agents SDK | 19k | 10.3M | • Lightweight design\u003cbr/\u003e• Multi-agent workflows\u003cbr/\u003e• 100+ LLM support | • Website-to-agent conversions\u003cbr/\u003e• Documentation assistants | Quick prototyping and general-purpose agents |\n| AutoGen | 54.6k | 856k | • Event-driven architecture\u003cbr/\u003e• GAIA benchmark leader\u003cbr/\u003e• Multi-agent conversations | • Novo Nordisk: Data science workflows\u003cbr/\u003e• Merged into Microsoft Agent Framework | Complex multi-agent systems and data science |\n| CrewAI | 44.3k | 5.2M | • Role-based agents\u003cbr/\u003e• Simple implementation\u003cbr/\u003e• Streaming tool calls (Jan 2026) | • Customer service bots\u003cbr/\u003e• Marketing automation | Quick agent deployment without complex dependencies |\n| Google ADK | 17.8k | 3.3M | • Google ecosystem integration\u003cbr/\u003e• Hierarchical compositions\u003cbr/\u003e• Custom tools | • Google
