For web-sourced PDFs specifically, Firecrawl's [document extraction](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/pdf-parser-v2) handles scanned documents and complex layouts before they reach your chunking pipeline.\n\n### Trade-offs\n\nPros:\n\n- Preserves page context and visual layout\n- Handles tables and figures naturally\n- Works with documents where pages represent logical units\n- Highest accuracy in NVIDIA benchmarks (0.648 with lowest variance)\n\nCons:\n\n- Only makes sense for paginated documents (PDFs, presentations)\n- Assumes pages align with semantic boundaries (not always true)\n- Variable chunk sizes based on page content density\n- May create very small or very large chunks depending on pagination\n\n### Research validation\n\nNVIDIA's 2024 benchmark tested seven chunking strategies across five datasets. Page-level chunking won with 0.648 accuracy and the lowest standard deviation (0.107), meaning it performed consistently well across different document types.\n\nThe results make sense. Financial reports, legal documents, and research papers organize information by pages. Respecting that structure helps retrieval find the right context.\n\nBut remember: NVIDIA tested document types where pagination matters. If your PDFs are just text exports with arbitrary page breaks, page-level chunking won't help.\n\n## Semantic chunking\n\nSemantic chunking splits text based on meaning, not structure. Instead of looking for paragraph breaks or sentence boundaries, it analyzes how related consecutive sentences are and creates chunks where topics shift.\n\n### How it works\n\nThe process follows four steps:\n\n1. **Sentence segmentation**: Break the document into individual sentences\n2. **Embedding generation**: Create vector embeddings for each sentence\n3. **Similarity analysis**: Compare embeddings between consecutive sentences to measure how related they are\n4. **Chunk formation**: When similarity drops below a threshold, start a new chunk\n\nConsider a research paper. The introduction flows naturally from background to motivation to contribution. Then there's a shift to related work. That transition is noticeable when reading. Semantic chunking detects it mathematically using embedding similarity.\n\nThe threshold determines sensitivity. Three common methods exist:\n\n**Percentile threshold (default)**: Split when similarity difference exceeds the 95th percentile. If most sentences are 0.85 similar but two consecutive ones are only 0.65 similar, that's a topic shift.\n\n**Standard deviation**: Split when difference exceeds 3 standard deviations from the mean. Catches statistically unusual topic transitions.\n\n**Interquartile range**: Uses the middle 50% of similarity scores to identify outliers. Less sensitive to extreme values than standard deviation.\n\n### Implementation example\n\nLangChain's `SemanticChunker` handles the embedding and similarity analysis:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker\nfrom langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\nimport os\n\n# Set up embeddings model\nembeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(\n    model=\"text-embedding-3-small\",\n    openai_api_key=os.getenv(\"OPENAI_API_KEY\")\n)\n\n# Load document, see the Getting Sample Data section to scrape your own drug_info_00 dataset with Firecrawl.\ndoc_path = Path(\"data/raw_documents/drug_info_00.json\")\nwith open(doc_path) as f:\n    doc = json.load(f)\n\n# Configure semantic chunker\nsemantic_splitter = SemanticChunker(\n    embeddings=embeddings,\n    breakpoint_threshold_type=\"percentile\",  # or \"standard_deviation\", \"interquartile\"\n    breakpoint_threshold_amount=95\n)\n\n# Split based on semantic similarity\nchunks = semantic_splitter.split_text(doc[\"markdown\"])\n\n# Show results\nprint(f\"Document: {doc['title']}\")\nprint(f\"Number of chunks: {len(chunks)}\")\nprint(f\"\\nChunk sizes:\")\nfor i, chunk in enumerate(chunks[:5]):\n    print(f\"Chunk {i}: {len(chunk)} characters\")\n```\n\nWe initialize OpenAI embeddings, load our document, and configure a semantic chunker that uses the percentile threshold method (95th percentile) to detect topic transitions. The chunker embeds each sentence, compares consecutive embeddings, and splits where similarity drops sharply.\n\nThe results show highly variable chunk sizes based on semantic coherence rather than fixed limits:\n\n```plaintext\nDocument: Sertraline (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\nNumber of chunks: 8\n\nChunk sizes:\nChunk 0: 1811 characters\nChunk 1: 315 characters\nChunk 2: 4701 characters\nChunk 3: 8405 characters\nChunk 4: 6309 characters\n```\n\nNotice the fewer chunks compared to size-based or recursive splitting. Semantic chunking groups related content together regardless of length. Each chunk represents a coherent topic.\n\n### When to use this\n\nSemantic chunking works best for:\n\n- Dense unstructured text (research papers, long-form articles, technical documentation)\n- Content with subtle topic transitions that structure-based methods miss\n- When retrieval accuracy is the top priority and budget allows\n- Documents where natural sections aren't marked with headers\n\nThe cost matters. Every sentence needs an embedding, which means API calls (if using OpenAI) or local model inference. For a 10,000-word document, you might generate 200-300 embeddings just for chunking.\n\n### Trade-offs\n\nPros:\n\n- Maintains semantic coherence across chunk boundaries\n- Detects subtle topic shifts that structure-based methods miss\n- 2-3 percentage points better recall than RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (Chroma research found performance differences up to 9 percentage points across all chunking methods tested)\n- Highest accuracy: 0.919 recall with LLM-enhanced variant\n\nCons:\n\n- Computationally expensive (embedding every sentence)\n- Requires threshold tuning for your specific content\n- Embedding API costs add up (or local model inference time)\n- Slower processing than simpler methods\n- A [NAACL 2025 Findings paper](https://aclanthology.org/2025.findings-naacl.114/) concluded the computational costs aren't justified by consistent gains, with fixed 200-word chunks matching or beating semantic chunking across retrieval and answer generation tasks\n\n### Advanced variants\n\nCluster semantic chunking groups similar sentences even if they aren't adjacent. This helps when a document revisits topics or has nested structure. This is a more advanced approach than standard semantic chunking. [LlamaIndex's SemanticSplitterNodeParser](https://developers.llamaindex.ai/python/examples/node_parsers/semantic_chunking/) implements standard semantic splitting by analyzing consecutive sentences.\n\nHierarchical chunking creates multiple chunk layers. Summary chunks for high-level queries, detail chunks for specific questions. More complex but powerful for documents with nested information architecture.\n\nTest whether semantic chunking justifies the cost for your use case. If recursive splitting gives 88% recall and semantic gives 91%, is the 3% improvement worth 10x processing time and embedding costs?\n\n## LLM-based chunking\n\nLLM-based chunking uses a language model to analyze document structure and decide where to split. Instead of following fixed rules or embedding similarity, the LLM reads the content and makes context-aware decisions about chunk boundaries.\n\n### How it works\n\nYou send the document (or sections of it) to an LLM with instructions about how to chunk it. The model analyzes the content, identifies logical boundaries, and returns split points or pre-chunked content.\n\nA basic approach:\n\n1. **Send document sections to LLM**: \"Identify logical section breaks in this text where topics shift\"\n2. **LLM analyzes structure**: Understands headers, topic transitions, argument flow\n3. **Receive split points or chunks**: Get back either marked boundaries or pre-chunked text\n4. **Create final chunks**: Use LLM suggestions to split the original document\n\nMore sophisticated approaches ask the LLM to summarize each chunk or generate metadata, creating semantically rich chunks with built-in context.\n\n### Implementation example\n\nHere's a conceptual example using OpenAI's API:\n\n```python\nfrom openai import OpenAI\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nclient = OpenAI()\n\n# Load document, see the Getting Sample Data section to scrape your own drug_info_00 dataset with Firecrawl.\ndoc_path = Path(\"data/raw_documents/drug_info_00.json\")\nwith open(doc_path) as f:\n    doc = json.load(f)\n\n# Prompt LLM to identify chunk boundaries\nresponse = client.chat.completions.create(\n    model=\"gpt-4\",\n    messages=[{\n        \"role\": \"system\",\n        \"content\": \"You are a document analysis expert. Identify logical sections in the following medical document and suggest where to split it into semantically coherent chunks. Return section titles and approximate character positions for splits.\"\n    }, {\n        \"role\": \"user\",\n        \"content\": doc[\"markdown\"][:8000]  # Send first portion\n    }]\n)\n\n# Parse LLM suggestions\nsuggestions = response.choices[0].message.content\nprint(\"LLM-suggested chunks:\")\nprint(suggestions)\n\n# In production, you'd parse the response and create actual chunks\n# This is a simplified example showing the concept\n```\n\nWe send the first 8000 characters of our document to GPT-4 with a system prompt instructing it to analyze document structure and suggest chunk boundaries. The LLM returns section titles with character positions.\n\nIn a production system, you'd parse these suggestions and use them to split the full document. Here's example output:\n\n```plaintext\nLLM-suggested chunks:\n1. \"Table of Contents\" (Start: 0, End: 777)\n2. \"Brand Name\" (Start: 780, End: 869)\n3. \"Description\" (Start: 872, End: 1518)\n4. \"Before Using\" (Start: 1521, End: 6835)\n5. \"Proper Use\" (Start: 6838, End: 7680)\n```\n\nThe LLM identified semantic sections that align with how medical professionals think about drug information, not just where paragraph breaks happen.\n\n### When to use this\n\nLLM-based chunking makes sense for:\n\n- High-value content where chunking quality directly affects business outcomes\n- Documents with complex or unusual structure that breaks simple methods\n- Experimental projects where you can iterate on prompts\n- Small document collections where processing cost isn't prohibitive\n\nThis isn't for production-scale document processing unless cost isn't a concern. One 10,000-word document might need multiple LLM calls to analyze fully, each costing $0.01-$0.10 depending on model and token count.\n\n### Trade-offs\n\nPros:\n\n- Context-aware decisions based on actual content meaning\n- Adapts to document structure dynamically\n- Can generate chunk summaries and metadata in the same pass\n- Handles unusual document types that break rule-based methods\n\nCons:\n\n- Expensive (LLM API costs for every document)\n- Slow (LLM inference latency, especially for large documents)\n- Requires LLM access (API dependency or local model infrastructure)\n- Limited production use (cost and latency prohibitive at scale)\n- Needs prompt engineering and testing\n\n### Agentic chunking\n\nAgentic chunking extends the LLM approach by giving the model agency to decide chunking strategy per document. Instead of one fixed prompt, the agent analyzes document characteristics and picks the right method.\n\nA research paper might get semantic chunking. A financial report might get page-level. A code file might get function-level splitting. The agent decides based on document type, structure, and content density.\n\nThis sounds promising, but it remains largely experimental. [Weaviate's chunking blog](https://weaviate.io/blog/chunking-strategies-for-rag) and [F22 Labs guide](https://www.f22labs.com/blogs/7-chunking-strategies-in-rag-you-need-to-know/) discuss the approach, though the complexity and cost limit real-world adoption.\n\n### Practical considerations\n\nIf you're considering LLM-based chunking, test it on a small sample first. Calculate actual costs:\n\n- 100 documents × 5,000 words each = 500,000 words\n- At ~1.3 tokens/word = 650,000 tokens\n- GPT-4 input: $5.00/1M tokens = $3.25\n- Add output tokens and multiple passes for large docs\n\nFor ongoing document processing, costs add up fast. LLM-based chunking works best for one-time processing of valuable content or as a benchmark to evaluate simpler methods.\n\n## Late chunking\n\nEvery strategy covered so far shares the same assumption: you split the document first, then embed each chunk on its own. Late chunking, [introduced by Jina AI in 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.04701), flips that order. The transformer processes the full document before any splitting happens, so each chunk's embedding carries context from the entire text.\n\n### How it works\n\nConsider a chunk containing \"Its population exceeds 3.85 million\" where \"Berlin\" appeared three paragraphs earlier, now sitting in a different chunk. With standard chunking, the self-attention layers only see one chunk at a time, so that pronoun reference is lost.\n\nLate chunking runs the full document through the transformer first. At this point, every token embedding reflects bidirectional attention over the entire input, and the token for \"Its\" already encodes information about \"Berlin\" from the broader context. Only after this forward pass do you apply chunk boundaries and mean-pool the token embeddings within each span to produce the final chunk vectors.\n\nIn practice, you tokenize the full document up to the model's context window, run the complete token sequence through all transformer layers, then map your chunk boundaries onto the resulting token-level embeddings. Those boundaries can come from any strategy covered in this article: recursive splitting, sentence-based, fixed-size, or semantic. The final step is mean-pooling within each chunk span to get one vector per chunk. The [paper's benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.04701) show retrieval improved across all boundary strategies when paired with late chunking.\n\nThis means late chunking isn't a replacement for other strategies, but a layer on top of them.\n\n### Implementation example\n\nThe most practical path today is [Jina's embedding API](https://jina.ai/embeddings/), which supports late chunking as a parameter. When `late_chunking` is set to `True`, the API concatenates all input strings, processes them through the transformer as a single sequence, then returns one embedding per string.\n\n```python\nimport requests\nimport os\n\nurl = \"https://api.jina.ai/v1/embeddings\"\nheaders = {\n    \"Content-Type\": \"application/json\",\n    \"Authorization\": f\"Bearer {os.getenv('JINA_API_KEY')}\",\n}\n\n# Pass chunks as separate strings in the input array\n# The API concatenates them internally for the full-document forward pass\ndata = {\n    \"input\": [\n        \"Berlin is the capital and largest city of Germany, both by area and by population.\",\n        \"Its more than 3.85 million inhabitants make it the European Union's most populous city.\",\n        \"The city is also one of the states of Germany, and is the third smallest state by area.\",\n    ],\n    \"model\": \"jina-embeddings-v3\",\n    \"task\": \"retrieval.passage\",\n    \"late_chunking\": True,\n}\n\nresponse = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=data)\nembeddings = response.json()[\"data\"]\n\nfor i, emb in enumerate(embeddings):\n    print(f\"Chunk {i}: {len(emb['embedding'])} dimensions\")\n```\n\nThe second chunk's embedding now knows \"Its\" refers to Berlin, because the transformer saw the full concatenated text before pooling.\n\nFor teams already using LangChain, the [`langchain-jina`](https://pypi.org/project/langchain-jina/) package wraps this into a familiar interface:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_jina import LateChunkEmbeddings\n\nembeddings = LateChunkEmbeddings(\n    jina_api_key=os.getenv(\"JINA_API_KEY\"),\n    model_name=\"jina-embeddings-v3\"\n)\n\nvectors = embeddings.embed_documents([\n    \"Berlin is the capital and largest city of Germany.\",\n    \"Its more than 3.85 million inhabitants make it the EU's most populous city.\",\n])\n```\n\nYou can also run late chunking locally with Jina's [open source implementation](https://github.com/jina-ai/late-chunking) using any HuggingFace model that supports mean pooling.\n\n### When to use this\n\nLate chunking fits documents where chunks frequently reference information from other parts of the text: legal contracts with cross-references, research papers where the methods section depends on definitions from the introduction, or technical documentation heavy on pronouns and acronyms introduced earlier.\n\nThe performance gap widens with document length. On NFCorpus (average 1,589 characters per document), late chunking improved nDCG@10 by 6.5 points over naive chunking. On Quora (average 62 characters), there was zero improvement because short texts don't have cross-chunk dependencies to preserve.\n\n### Trade-offs\n\nPros:\n\n- Chunk embeddings carry full-document context without extra API calls or inference steps beyond what you'd already run for embedding\n- Works as a layer on top of any boundary strategy (recursive, sentence-based, fixed-size)\n- Approached the quality of LLM-augmented contextual embeddings in the paper's tests (0.8516 vs 0.8590 cosine similarity) at a fraction of the cost\n\nCons:\n\n- Requires models with mean pooling and long context windows; CLS-token models like some BERT variants won't work\n- The entire document must fit in the model's context window (8,192 tokens for [jina-embeddings-v3](https://jina.ai/embeddings/), roughly 10 pages)\n- The Jina API is the only production-ready path right now, though local usage is possible through the [transformers library](https://github.com/jina-ai/late-chunking)\n- A weaker embedding model with late chunking still underperforms a stronger model without it\n\n## What the research says about chunking strategies for LLMs and RAG\n\n[NVIDIA tested seven chunking strategies](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/finding-the-best-chunking-strategy-for-accurate-ai-responses/) across five datasets in 2024. Page-level chunking won with 0.648 accuracy and 0.107 standard deviation. Query type affected optimal chunk size: factoid queries performed best with 256-512 tokens, analytical queries needed 1024+ tokens.\n\n[Chroma Research found](https://research.trychroma.com/evaluating-chunking) performance varied by up to 9% in recall across methods. LLMSemanticChunker achieved 0.919 recall, ClusterSemanticChunker reached 0.913, and RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter hit 85.4-89.5% (best at 400 tokens: 88.1-89.5%).\n\n[Superlinked's HotpotQA tests](https://superlinked.com/vectorhub/articles/evaluation-rag-retrieval-chunking-methods) showed SentenceSplitter outperformed semantic approaches with ColBERT v2 embeddings. Embedding model choice matters as much as chunking strategy.\n\nMore recent research reinforces some of these patterns and challenges others. A [peer-reviewed clinical decision support study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12649634/) (MDPI Bioengineering, November 2025) found that adaptive chunking aligned to logical topic boundaries hit 87% accuracy versus 13% for fixed-size baselines, with the gap statistically confirmed at p = 0.001. [Vecta's February 2026 benchmark](https://www.runvecta.com/blog/we-benchmarked-7-chunking-strategies-most-advice-was-wrong) of 7 strategies across 50 academic papers placed recursive 512-token splitting first at 69% accuracy, while semantic chunking landed at 54% after producing fragments averaging just 43 tokens.\n\nOn context length, Chroma's [context rot research](https://research.trychroma.com/context-rot) (July 2025) tested 18 models including GPT-4.1, Claude 4, and Gemini 2.5 and found that retrieval performance degrades as context length increases, even on straightforward tasks. A [January 2026 systematic analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.14123) identified a \"context cliff\" around 2,500 tokens where response quality drops, and separately found that sentence chunking matched semantic chunking up to ~5,000 tokens at a fraction of the cost.\n\nStart with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 400-512 tokens with 10-20% overlap. Move to semantic or page-level chunking only if your metrics show you need the extra performance and budget allows for the costs.\n\n## Decision framework\n\n### The default choice\n\nStart with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter:\n\n- Chunk size: 400-512 tokens\n- Overlap: 50-100 tokens (10-20%)\n- Separators: `[\"\\n\\n\", \"\\n\", \" \", \"\"]` (default) or add `\". \"` for better sentence splitting\n\nThis handles most text content well: blog posts, documentation, research papers, web articles.\n\n### When to use a different approach\n\nYou can also combine strategies based on content type. Here's when to pick something else:\n\n| Your Situation                       | Use This Strategy                              | Why                                                      |\n| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |\n| Working with PDFs                    | Page-level chunking (details below)            | Won NVIDIA's benchmarks (0.648 accuracy), handles tables |\n| Accuracy is important, budget allows | Semantic chunking (details below)              | Up to 9% better recall, maintains semantic coherence     |\n| Budget is tight, need speed          | Size-based chunking (details below)            | Fastest to implement, no computational overhead          |\n| Processing code files                | Recursive with code separators (details below) | Respects function/class boundaries                       |\n| Short-form content (tweets, Q\u0026A)     | Sentence-based chunking (details below)        | Preserves complete thoughts                              |\n| High-value content, experimental     | LLM-based chunking (details below)             | Context-aware, adapts to structure dynamically           |\n\n**Many production systems use hybrid approaches:** route PDFs to page-level chunking, web pages to recursive splitting, and code to code-aware separators based on file type or content analysis.\n\n### Adjust chunk size for your query type\n\nOnce you've picked a strategy, tune the chunk size:\n\n- **Factoid queries** (names, dates, facts): 256-512 tokens for precise matching\n- **Analytical queries** (explanations, comparisons): 1024+ tokens for more context\n- **Mixed queries**: Start with 400-512 tokens (balanced middle ground)\n\n**Conflicting signals?** If your content type suggests one approach (PDFs → page-level) but query type suggests another (factoid → 256-512 tokens), content type usually wins. Test both to confirm.\n\nBefore going to production, test 2-3 strategies on 50-100 representative documents with 20-30 realistic queries. Measure recall, precision, and answer quality to pick the winner for your use case.\n\n## Getting sample data for testing chunking strategies\n\nTesting chunking strategies requires sample data. For this article, we'll use medical and pharmaceutical content because it contains the variety that chunking needs to handle: technical terminology, structured sections, dosage tables, and regulatory information. The challenge is getting this web content clean content without HTML noise.\n\nScraping websites typically gives you HTML mixed with navigation menus, forms, JavaScript elements, and other artifacts. This noise fragments during chunking and pollutes your embeddings. Clean extraction solves this problem.\n\n### Building a test dataset with Firecrawl\n\nFirecrawl extracts clean content from web pages. It renders JavaScript, removes boilerplate elements, and converts everything to markdown or structured JSON while preserving headers, structured data, and document hierarchy.\n\nWe'll build a test dataset by scraping drug information from Mayo Clinic. Their drug reference pages provide technical depth, clear section structure (Description, Before Using, Proper Use, Precautions, Side Effects), and real-world complexity for testing chunking approaches.\n\nThe process uses two Firecrawl methods:\n\n1. Map to discover drug information URLs from the site\n2. Batch scrape to extract clean content from those pages\n\n### Setup\n\nFirst, sign up at [firecrawl.dev](https://firecrawl.dev/) and get your API key. Install the SDK:\n\n```bash\npip install firecrawl-py python-dotenv\n```\n\nSave your API key in a `.env` file:\n\n```bash\ntouch .env\necho \"FIRECRAWL_API_KEY='fc-YOUR-KEY-HERE'\" \u003e\u003e .env\n```\n\n### Discovering and scraping drug information\n\nWith Firecrawl configured, we'll use the [`map`](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/map) endpoint to discover drug information pages from Mayo Clinic, then [`batch_scrape`](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/batch-scrape) to extract their content in parallel:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nload_dotenv()\napp = Firecrawl()\n\n# Step 1: Discover drug information pages with map\nmap_result = app.map(\n    \"https://www.mayoclinic.org/drugs-supplements\",\n    search=\"drug information oral route\",\n    limit=10\n)\n\n# Filter out the index page\ndrug_urls = [\n    link.url for link in map_result.links\n    if link.url != \"https://www.mayoclinic.org/drugs-supplements\"\n]\n\nprint(f\"Discovered {len(drug_urls)} drug information pages\")\n```\n\nThe `map` endpoint generates a list of URLs from a site without scraping full page content. The `search` parameter filters results by relevance, so we get drug information pages rather than navigation or category pages.\n\nWith URLs in hand, `batch_scrape` fetches all pages concurrently:\n\n```python\n# Step 2: Batch scrape all discovered drug pages\nbatch_result = app.batch_scrape(drug_urls, formats=[\"markdown\"])\n\n# Process results\ndocuments = []\nfor doc in batch_result.data:\n    documents.append({\n        \"url\": doc.metadata.url if doc.metadata else \"\",\n        \"markdown\": doc.markdown,\n        \"title\": doc.metadata.title if doc.metadata else \"\",\n        \"description\": doc.metadata.description if doc.metadata else \"\"\n    })\n    print(f\"Scraped: {doc.metadata.title if doc.metadata else 'Unknown'}\")\n```\n\nEach result contains clean markdown with the drug information content, preserving sections like Description, Before Using, Proper Use, Precautions, and Side Effects. Save the documents to disk for chunking experiments:\n\n```python\n# Save to disk for chunking experiments\noutput_dir = Path(\"data/raw_documents\")\noutput_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\nfor i, doc in enumerate(documents):\n    filepath = output_dir / f\"drug_info_{i:02d}.json\"\n    with open(filepath, \"w\") as f:\n        json.dump(doc, f, indent=2)\n\nprint(f\"\\nSaved {len(documents)} documents to {output_dir}\")\n```\n\nOutput:\n\n```plaintext\nDiscovered 13 drug information pages\nScraped: Tramadol (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\nScraped: Tadalafil (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\nScraped: Metformin (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\nScraped: Ibuprofen (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\nScraped: Oxycodone (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic\n\nSaved 13 documents to data/raw_documents\n```\n\n### Beyond scraping: agent, browser, and CLI\n\nThe `scrape`, `crawl`, `batch_scrape`, and `map` endpoints cover most data collection needs for chunking pipelines. Firecrawl also has a few features worth knowing about for more complex workflows.\n\nThe [`/agent` endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) handles cases where you need structured data from the web without writing scraping logic. You describe what you want in plain text, optionally pass a Pydantic schema, and the agent searches, navigates, and returns structured results:\n\n```python\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel, Field\nfrom typing import List, Optional\n\nclass DrugInfo(BaseModel):\n    name: str = Field(description=\"Drug name\")\n    common_uses: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"Primary uses\")\n    drug_class: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"Drug classification\")\n\nclass DrugList(BaseModel):\n    drugs: List[DrugInfo] = Field(description=\"List of drugs\")\n\nresult = app.agent(\n    prompt=\"Find 3 commonly prescribed oral medications on Mayo Clinic and their primary uses\",\n    schema=DrugList,\n    model=\"spark-1-mini\",\n)\n\nprint(result.data)\n```\n\n```plaintext\n{\n  \"drugs\": [\n    {\"name\": \"Atorvastatin\", \"common_uses\": \"Lower high cholesterol, reduce risk of heart attack and stroke\", \"drug_class\": \"HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor (statin)\"},\n    {\"name\": \"Lisinopril\", \"common_uses\": \"Treat high blood pressure and heart failure\", \"drug_class\": \"ACE inhibitor\"},\n    {\"name\": \"Levothyroxine\", \"common_uses\": \"Treat hypothyroidism, decrease enlarged thyroid glands\", \"drug_class\": \"Thyroid hormone\"}\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nThe agent comes in two models: `spark-1-mini` (default, 60% cheaper) for straightforward extractions, and `spark-1-pro` for complex multi-domain research.\n\nThe [Browser Sandbox](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/browser) gives agents a secure, managed browser environment with Playwright pre-installed. Each session runs in an isolated sandbox where you can execute Python, JavaScript, or bash commands remotely:\n\n```python\nsession = app.browser(ttl=120)\nprint(session.cdp_url)  # wss://browser.firecrawl.dev/cdp/...\n\nresult = app.browser_execute(\n    session.id,\n    code='await page.goto(\"https://news.ycombinator.com\")\\ntitle = await page.title()\\nprint(title)',\n    language=\"python\",\n)\n\nprint(result.stdout)  # \"Hacker News\"\napp.delete_browser(session.id)\n```\n\nFor terminal-based workflows, the [Firecrawl CLI](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/sdks/cli) supports scraping, searching, crawling, and running the agent from the command line:\n\n```bash\n# Scrape a page to markdown\nfirecrawl https://example.com --format markdown --only-main-content\n\n# Discover URLs from a site\nfirecrawl map https://example.com --search \"blog\" --limit 100\n\n# Run the agent\nfirecrawl agent \"Find top 5 AI startups\" --wait\n```\n\n### Why clean extraction matters for chunking\n\nThe difference between raw HTML and Firecrawl's markdown becomes clear when you chunk:\n\n**Raw HTML:** Character-based splitting breaks `\u003cdiv\u003e` tags mid-element, fragments dosage tables around form elements, and embeds navigation text in your chunks.\n\n**Firecrawl markdown:** Section headers (Description, Before Using, Proper Use, Precautions, Side Effects) provide natural split points. Dosage tables stay intact. Medical terminology is properly formatted. Every chunking strategy works better with this clean input.\n\nWith clean data ready, you can now test chunking strategies on the same content and make fair comparisons. The next section starts with recursive character splitting, the recommended default approach for most text content.\n\n## Complete RAG pipeline example\n\nYou've seen different chunking strategies and how they work in isolation. Here's how chunking fits into a complete RAG pipeline.\n\nA typical RAG workflow follows these steps: collect data, clean it, chunk it, embed it, store it in a vector database, and query. We already covered data collection in the [Getting Sample Data](#getting-sample-data-for-testing-chunking-strategies) section where we scraped drug information with Firecrawl. Now we'll build the rest of the pipeline, focusing on how the chunking step affects the final system.\n\n### The workflow\n\nHere's what a complete pipeline looks like:\n\n1. Scrape web content with Firecrawl (covered in section 4)\n2. Load the clean markdown\n3. Chunk the documents (strategy choice matters here)\n4. Generate embeddings\n5. Store in your vector database\n6. Query\n\nWe'll build this pipeline with Pinecone and recursive character splitting. You'll see how to swap in different chunking strategies or vector databases later. If you haven't chosen a vector database yet, see our [comparison of 18 options](/blog/best-vector-databases-2025) covering Pinecone, Qdrant, Milvus, pgvector, and more.\n\n### Building the pipeline with recursive chunking\n\nWe'll use the drug information we scraped earlier, chunk it with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, and store it in Pinecone. First, install the required packages:\n\n```bash\npip install langchain-pinecone langchain-openai pinecone-client langchain-text-splitters python-dotenv\n```\n\nSet up your environment variables in a `.env` file:\n\n```bash\nPINECONE_API_KEY=your-pinecone-key\nOPENAI_API_KEY=your-openai-key\n```\n\nThe code below builds the complete pipeline. We'll walk through each step.\n\n#### Import dependencies and load environment\n\n```python\nimport os\nimport json\nfrom pathlib import Path\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nfrom pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec\nfrom langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings\nfrom langchain_pinecone import PineconeVectorStore\nfrom langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter\nfrom langchain_core.documents import Document\n\nload_dotenv()\n```\n\nThis imports libraries for vector storage (Pinecone), embeddings (OpenAI), and document chunking (LangChain).\n\n#### Initialize Pinecone and create index\n\n```python\n# Initialize Pinecone\npc = Pinecone(api_key=os.environ[\"PINECONE_API_KEY\"])\nindex_name = \"drug-info-rag\"\n\n# Create index if it doesn't exist\nif not pc.has_index(index_name):\n    pc.create_index(\n        name=index_name,\n        dimension=1536,\n        metric=\"cosine\",\n        spec=ServerlessSpec(cloud=\"aws\", region=\"us-east-1\")\n    )\n```\n\nThis connects to Pinecone and creates a serverless index configured for OpenAI's `text-embedding-3-small` model (1536 dimensions) using cosine similarity.\n\n#### Set up embeddings and vector store\n\n```python\n# Set up vector store\nembeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model=\"text-embedding-3-small\")\nindex = pc.Index(index_name)\nvector_store = PineconeVectorStore(index=index, embedding=embeddings)\n```\n\nThis initializes the OpenAI embeddings model and connects it to our Pinecone index through LangChain's vector store wrapper.\n\n#### Load documents\n\n```python\n# Load the scraped drug information\ndocuments = []\ndata_dir = Path(\"data/raw_documents\")\n\nfor json_file in data_dir.glob(\"drug_info_*.json\"):\n    with open(json_file) as f:\n        doc_data = json.load(f)\n        documents.append(\n            Document(\n                page_content=doc_data[\"markdown\"],\n                metadata={\"source\": doc_data[\"title\"], \"url\": doc_data[\"url\"]}\n            )\n        )\n\nprint(f\"Loaded {len(documents)} documents\")\n```\n\nThis reads the JSON files containing our scraped drug information and converts them to LangChain `Document` objects, preserving the title and URL as metadata.\n\n#### Chunk documents with recursive splitting\n\n```python\n# Chunk with recursive character splitter\nsplitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(\n    chunk_size=500,\n    chunk_overlap=50,\n    separators=[\"\\n\\n\", \"\\n\", \". \", \" \", \"\"]\n)\n\nchunks = splitter.split_documents(documents)\nprint(f\"Created {len(chunks)} chunks from {len(documents)} documents\")\nprint(f\"Average chunk size: {sum(len(c.page_content) for c in chunks) // len(chunks)} characters\")\n```\n\nChunking strategy matters here. We're using recursive character splitting with 500-character chunks and 50-character overlap. The separator hierarchy (`[\"\\n\\n\", \"\\n\", \". \", \" \", \"\"]`) means the splitter tries paragraph breaks first, then line breaks, then sentence boundaries, then word boundaries. This preserves the document structure we got from Firecrawl's clean markdown.\n\nThe output shows how many chunks we created and their average size.\n\n#### Add to Pinecone and query\n\n```python\n# Add chunks to Pinecone\nvector_store.add_documents(chunks)\nprint(\"Added chunks to Pinecone\")\n\n# Query the system\nquery = \"What are the side effects of sertraline?\"\nresults = vector_store.similarity_search(query, k=3)\n\nprint(f\"\\nQuery: '{query}'\\n\")\nfor i, result in enumerate(results, 1):\n    print(f\"Result {i} (from {result.metadata['source']}):\")\n    print(result.page_content[:200] + \"...\\n\")\n```\n\nThis uploads all chunks to Pinecone with their embeddings, then performs a semantic search for information about sertraline side effects. The system retrieves the top 3 most similar chunks.\n\n**Output:**\n\n```plaintext\nLoaded 9 documents\nCreated 127 chunks from 9 documents\nAverage chunk size: 456 characters\nAdded chunks to Pinecone\n\nQuery: 'What are the side effects of sertraline?'\n\nResult 1 (from Sertraline (oral route) - Side effects \u0026 dosage - Mayo Clinic):\n## Side effects\n\nAlong with its needed effects, a medicine may cause some unwanted effects. Although not all of these side effects may occur, if they do occur they may need medical attention.\n\nCheck with your doctor...\n```\n\nThe chunking strategy directly affected these results. Because we used recursive splitting with sentence boundaries, each chunk contains complete thoughts about side effects. The 500-character size was large enough to capture multiple related side effects in each chunk, giving the LLM good context for answering the question.\n\n### Testing different chunking strategies\n\nYou can test how chunking strategy affects retrieval by changing just the splitter configuration. Here's semantic chunking instead:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker\n\n# Replace the RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with SemanticChunker\nsemantic_splitter = SemanticChunker(\n    embeddings=embeddings,\n    breakpoint_threshold_type=\"percentile\"\n)\n\nsemantic_chunks = semantic_splitter.split_documents(documents)\nprint(f\"Semantic chunking created {len(semantic_chunks)} chunks\")\n```\n\nSemantic chunking will create fewer, larger chunks because it groups sentences by semantic similarity rather than character count. You'd then add these chunks to a different Pinecone index and compare retrieval quality for your specific queries.\n\nThe same pattern works for other strategies: swap the splitter, re-chunk, compare results.\n\n### Adapting for other vector databases\n\nThe pattern stays the same across all [vector databases](/blog/best-vector-databases-2025): load data, chunk it, embed it, store it. Only the vector store setup changes.\n\nFor **Qdrant**:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_qdrant import QdrantVectorStore\nfrom qdrant_client import QdrantClient\n\nclient = QdrantClient(url=\"http://localhost:6333\")\nvector_store = QdrantVectorStore(\n    client=client,\n    collection_name=\"drug-info\",\n    embedding=embeddings\n)\nvector_store.add_documents(chunks)\n```\n\nFor **Weaviate**:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_weaviate import WeaviateVectorStore\nimport weaviate\n\nwith weaviate.connect_to_local() as client:\n    vector_store = WeaviateVectorStore(\n        client=client,\n        index_name=\"DrugInfo\",\n        text_key=\"text\",\n        embedding=embeddings\n    )\n    vector_store.add_documents(chunks)\n```\n\nFor **ChromaDB**:\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_chroma import Chroma\n\nvector_store = Chroma(\n    collection_name=\"drug-info\",\n    embedding_function=embeddings,\n    persist_directory=\"./chroma_db\"\n)\nvector_store.add_documents(chunks)\n```\n\nFor **pgvector** (PostgreSQL):\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_postgres import PGVector\n\nconnection_string = \"postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/vectordb\"\nvector_store = PGVector(\n    connection_string=connection_string,\n    collection_name=\"drug_info\",\n    embedding_function=embeddings\n)\nvector_store.add_documents(chunks)\n```\n\nThe chunking logic stays identical across all of these. You use the same `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` (or any other strategy) and the same chunks. The vector database handles storage and retrieval, but the chunking quality determines what gets stored.\n\nThis is why chunking strategy matters more than vector database choice for many applications. A well-chunked document retrieves better regardless of which database you use. Poor chunking degrades retrieval even on the fastest vector database.\n\n## Which chunking strategy should you use?\n\nNo single chunking strategy is universally best. Page-level chunking won NVIDIA's benchmarks with 0.648 accuracy and the lowest variance across document types. Semantic chunking can improve recall by up to 9% over simpler methods. RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with 400-512 tokens delivered 85-90% recall in Chroma's tests without the computational overhead, making it a solid default for most teams.\n\nThe benchmarks in this article used specific datasets, embedding models, and query patterns that probably differ from yours. Your chunking choice determines what your vector database stores, which determines what your RAG system can retrieve. Track your metrics over time: recall shows whether you're retrieving relevant chunks, precision shows whether those chunks are actually useful, MRR and NDCG measure ranking quality.\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a6:T787c,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\n## TL;DR\n\n**Short on time? Here's what you need to know.**\n\nPick your category based on what matters most right now:\n\n| Category       | Top Pick          | Why                                                    |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |\n| All-in-One     | Langfuse          | Open source, MIT licensed, works with everything       |\n| Evaluation     | Arize Phoenix     | OpenTelemetry-native, no vendor lock-in                |\n| Gateway        | Portkey           | 250+ models, 20-40ms overhead, enterprise-ready        |\n| Enterprise APM | Use what you have | Datadog or New Relic if you're already paying for them |\n\n**Quick recommendations by use case:**\n\n- **RAG apps**: TruLens or Phoenix for retrieval quality metrics\n- **Agents**: Langfuse or LangSmith for multi-step debugging\n- **Chatbots**: Helicone for fast setup, Galileo for real-time guardrails\n- **Cost control**: Helicone or Portkey as your gateway layer\n\n**Open source options**: Langfuse (MIT), Phoenix, TruLens, Evidently, Helicone, Portkey, OpenLLMetry, and Weave all have open source versions. Opik is Apache 2.0. The rest are commercial with free tiers.\n\n**The 15 tools at a glance:**\n\n| Tool          | Category   | Open Source      | Best For                        |\n| ------------- | ---------- | ---------------- | ------------------------------- |\n| Langfuse      | All-in-One | Yes (MIT)        | Most teams starting out         |\n| LangSmith     | All-in-One | No               | LangChain users                 |\n| Braintrust    | All-in-One | No               | Prompt experimentation          |\n| Opik          | All-in-One | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Comet users, low-code platforms |\n| Confident AI  | Evaluation | No               | Evaluation-first observability  |\n| Arize Phoenix | Evaluation | Yes              | OpenTelemetry integration       |\n| TruLens       | Evaluation | Yes              | RAG quality metrics             |\n| Galileo AI    | Evaluation | No               | Real-time guardrails            |\n| Evidently AI  | Evaluation | Yes              | ML + LLM unified monitoring     |\n| Helicone      | Gateway    | Yes              | Fastest setup, caching          |\n| Portkey       | Gateway    | Yes              | Multi-provider routing          |\n| OpenLLMetry   | Gateway    | Yes              | Existing APM integration        |\n| Datadog       | Enterprise | No               | Datadog customers               |\n| New Relic     | Enterprise | No               | New Relic customers             |\n| W\u0026B Weave     | Enterprise | Yes              | MLOps experiment tracking       |\n\n---\n\nShipping an LLM app without observability is like debugging with print statements in production.\n\nYou'll waste hours on issues you can't reproduce and burn money on inefficient prompts. LLM observability tools solve this by giving you visibility into what's actually happening.\n\nLLMs are black boxes. Traditional logging captures requests and responses, but misses what matters: prompt variations, retrieval quality, and where things went wrong. What works in development often breaks in production.\n\nObservability tools give you visibility into the full pipeline. You can trace requests end-to-end, track costs, measure output quality, and catch problems before users report them.\n\nThis guide covers 15 tools across four categories: all-in-one platforms, evaluation-focused tools, gateway proxies, and enterprise APM extensions.\n\n## What is LLM observability?\n\nLLM observability is the practice of instrumenting your AI applications to understand their behavior in production. It covers the full pipeline from data ingestion (using tools like [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) for web data) through inference to output, letting you debug issues, optimize costs, and maintain quality at scale.\n\nThe term often gets confused with related concepts, so here's how they differ:\n\n**Monitoring** tracks real-time metrics like latency, token usage, costs, and error rates. This is your system health dashboard: alerts when things break, trends to track performance over time.\n\n**Evaluation** measures quality through accuracy testing, hallucination detection, and relevance scoring. It acts as your quality gate before deployment and your regression detector after.\n\n**Observability** provides complete request tracing that shows you why something failed. When monitoring flags an issue, observability helps you debug it by reconstructing the full chain of events, from input through reasoning to output.\n\nMost tools blend these capabilities rather than focusing on just one. The categories in this guide reflect that reality, helping you choose based on which capability matters most for your current needs.\n\n## What makes a great LLM observability tool?\n\nFor this article, we evaluated tools based on production readiness, active development, clear documentation, and reasonable pricing (or an open source option). Research projects and abandoned repos didn't make the cut.\n\nThe tools in this guide offer different combinations of these core capabilities:\n\n- **Tracing**: End-to-end request visibility across your entire pipeline, from prompt construction through model response\n- **Evaluation**: Built-in metrics for quality measurement, or the option to define custom evaluators for your use case\n- **Cost tracking**: Token usage monitoring and API spend aggregation to catch runaway costs before they hurt\n- **Integration**: Native support for your stack, whether that's LangChain, LlamaIndex, or vanilla API calls\n- **Self-hosting**: For teams with strict data requirements, the option to run on your own infrastructure\n\nNo single tool excels at all five. The categories below group tools by their primary strength, helping you match the tool to your most pressing need.\n\nA [CHI 2025 (Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3706599.3719914) study with 30 developers identified four core design principles every good LLM observability tool should address:\n\n- **Awareness** — makes model behavior visible so developers understand what's happening inside the system\n- **Monitoring** — provides real-time feedback during training and evaluation to catch issues early\n- **Intervention** — enables developers to act on problems as they surface, not after the fact\n- **Operability** — supports long-term maintainability as models and requirements evolve\n\nThe study also surfaced a useful tension: tools optimized for fast intervention tend to reduce developers' mental model clarity, while awareness-first designs deepen understanding but can slow response time. It's worth keeping in mind when deciding which tool fits your workflow.\n\n![CHI 2025 research paper on LLM observability design principles: awareness, monitoring, intervention, and operability](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/observability-research-paper.webp)\n\n## All-in-one platforms\n\n_Best for: Most teams starting out_\n\nThese platforms combine tracing, evaluation, and prompt management in a single package. If you're not sure where to start, pick one from this category.\n\n### Langfuse\n\n![Langfuse LLM observability platform dashboard showing traces, prompt management, and evaluation metrics](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/langfuse.webp)\n\n[Langfuse](https://langfuse.com/) is the open source leader in this space, with over [19,000 GitHub stars](https://github.com/langfuse/langfuse) and an MIT license that lets you self-host without restrictions. The platform covers the full observability stack: tracing with multi-turn conversation support, prompt versioning with a built-in playground, and flexible evaluation through LLM-as-judge, user feedback, or custom metrics.\n\nIntegration is straightforward. Native SDKs exist for Python and JavaScript, plus connectors for LangChain, LlamaIndex, and 50+ other frameworks. OpenTelemetry support means you can pipe traces into your existing observability stack if needed.\n\nThe cloud version has a generous free tier. Self-hosting is well-documented and actively maintained. The main trade-off: the UI is functional but less polished than some commercial alternatives.\n\n### LangSmith\n\n![LangSmith tracing and debugging interface for LangChain applications](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/langsmith.webp)\n\nIf you're already building with LangChain or [LangGraph](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/langgraph-startup-validator-tutorial), [LangSmith](https://www.langchain.com/langsmith) is the path of least resistance. Set one environment variable and tracing just works. The integration depth is unmatched: you get automatic instrumentation of chains, agents, and tool calls without touching your code.\n\nMain features:\n\n- Live dashboards for cost, latency, and response quality\n- Conversation clustering to surface common user intents\n- Agent-specific metrics showing tool popularity and error rates\n- Alerting when things break in production\n\nPricing starts free with 5,000 traces per month. The Plus tier runs \u0026#36;39 per user per month with 10,000 traces included. Enterprise customers can self-host on Kubernetes. The limitation: if you're not using [LangChain](https://www.langchain.com/), much of the value disappears. Framework-agnostic tracing exists but requires more setup.\n\n### Braintrust\n\n![Braintrust AI evaluation platform with experiment tracking and prompt comparison features](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/braintrust.webp)\n\n[Braintrust](https://www.braintrust.dev/) takes an evaluation-first approach. While it handles tracing and logging, the real strength is its experiment framework: define a dataset, run your prompt variations against it, and compare results side by side. This makes it ideal for teams who want to iterate on prompts systematically rather than debugging production issues.\n\nThe platform supports human review workflows alongside automated evals, and you can create datasets directly from production logs. Integrations cover the major providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) and frameworks (LangChain, OpenTelemetry). Self-hosting via Docker is available for teams with data residency requirements. Braintrust works best when evaluation is your primary concern; for pure observability, other tools offer more depth.\n\n### Opik\n\n![Opik LLM observability tool by Comet showing tracing and evaluation capabilities](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/opik.webp)\n\nBacked by [Comet](https://www.comet.com/) (the MLOps company), [Opik](https://www.comet.com/site/products/opik/) is the newest entrant but includes enterprise-focused features from day one. The feature set covers the full stack: tracing with cost tracking, built-in evaluation metrics (hallucination, moderation, relevance), prompt management with version control, and even an agent optimizer SDK for automated prompt tuning.\n\nWhat sets Opik apart is its breadth of integrations. Beyond the usual frameworks, it connects to low-code platforms like Dify and Flowise, making it accessible to teams building without heavy coding. Self-hosting options include both Docker Compose for small deployments and Kubernetes for scale. The [GitHub repo](https://github.com/comet-ml/opik) is open source under Apache 2.0.\n\nThe trade-off is maturity. As the newest platform, the community is smaller and documentation is still catching up. But for teams already using Comet for ML experiments, adding Opik creates a unified workflow.\n\n### Our top pick\n\n**Langfuse.** The combination of open source flexibility, active community, and production-ready features makes it the safest starting point for most teams. You can always migrate to something more specialized later.\n\n## Evaluation and testing\n\n_Best for: Quality-focused teams, guardrails_\n\nWhile the all-in-one platforms handle evaluation as one feature among many, these tools make it their primary focus. If catching hallucinations, measuring relevance, or enforcing guardrails matters most, you'll find more sophisticated capabilities here.\n\n### Confident AI\n\n![Confident AI LLM observability platform showing evaluation-first tracing and quality monitoring dashboard](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/confident-ai.webp)\n\n[Confident AI](https://www.confident-ai.com/) is an evaluation-first LLM observability platform built around [DeepEval](https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval), one of the most widely adopted open-source LLM evaluation frameworks. Rather than keeping evaluation as a separate step, it brings 50+ research-backed metrics directly into the observability layer — covering faithfulness, relevance, safety, and more.\n\nThe platform is OpenTelemetry-native with integrations for 10+ frameworks including LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Pydantic AI. Its standout feature is automatic dataset curation from production traces: failures surface directly in your evaluation datasets, closing the loop between what breaks in production and what you test next. Collaborative annotation workflows let non-technical teammates — product managers, QA, domain experts — review traces and run evaluations without involving engineering.\n\nThe platform is not open source, though enterprise self-hosting is available. Storage is priced at \u0026#36;1/GB-month with no limits on trace or span count. Paid tiers start at \u0026#36;19.99/seat/month.\n\n### Arize Phoenix\n\n![Arize Phoenix open source LLM evaluation tool with OpenTelemetry tracing and metrics](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/arize-phoenix.webp)\n\n[Arize Phoenix](https://phoenix.arize.com/) brings OpenTelemetry-native observability to LLM evaluation. Built by [Arize AI](https://arize.com/) and open source with [7,800+ GitHub stars](https://github.com/Arize-ai/phoenix), it accepts traces via the standard OTLP protocol, meaning you can integrate it into existing observability stacks without vendor lock-in.\n\nThe evaluation toolkit includes LLM-based evaluators, code-based metrics, and human annotation workflows. First-class instrumentation covers LangChain, LlamaIndex, DSPy, and direct API calls to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Bedrock. The prompt playground lets you test variations before committing changes.\n\nPhoenix runs locally for development and offers Phoenix Cloud for production. The trade-off: it's more observability-focused than pure evaluation, so teams wanting deep experiment management might need to look elsewhere.\n\n### TruLens\n\n![TruLens RAG evaluation framework showing feedback functions and quality metrics](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/trulens.webp)\n\nFor teams prioritizing RAG quality, [TruLens](https://www.trulens.org/) offers specialized evaluation through its feedback function framework. Now shepherded by [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) after their TruEra acquisition, the [open source library](https://github.com/truera/trulens) emits OpenTelemetry traces and evaluates them against built-in metrics: groundedness, context relevance, answer relevance, coherence, and toxicity detection.\n\nWhat makes TruLens stand out is programmatic evaluation of execution components. You can assess not just final outputs but retrieved context quality, tool call appropriateness, and agent planning decisions. Since [chunking strategy](/blog/best-chunking-strategies-rag) directly impacts retrieval quality, TruLens helps you measure whether your preprocessing choices actually work. Install via pip and run evaluations locally without sending data to external services.\n\nThe limitation is scope. TruLens focuses tightly on evaluation rather than full observability, so you'll likely pair it with another tool for tracing and monitoring.\n\n### Galileo AI\n\n![Galileo AI real-time LLM guardrails platform with Luna-2 evaluation models](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/galileo.webp)\n\n[Galileo](https://www.galileo.ai/) is a commercial LLM evaluation platform built around speed, with its Luna-2 evaluation models running at sub-200ms latency and roughly \u0026#36;0.02 per million tokens — making real-time guardrails economically viable even at scale. Unlike tools that prioritize flexibility or open standards, Galileo's core advantage is how fast it can intercept and score outputs in a live production environment.\n\nKey metrics include context adherence, tool selection quality, and conversation quality. The guardrail system can intercept risky agent actions before execution, blocking PII leaks or policy violations in real time. Luna-2 adapts to custom requirements with minimal labeled data, often fewer than 50 examples.\n\nThe platform announced a free tier for developers in 2025. For teams building customer-facing agents where latency and cost matter, Galileo's speed advantage matters. The downside: it's a commercial platform without a self-host option, so data leaves your infrastructure.\n\n### Evidently AI\n\n![Evidently AI unified ML and LLM evaluation platform with 100+ built-in metrics](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/evidentlyai.webp)\n\nIf your team runs both traditional ML models and LLM applications, [Evidently](https://www.evidentlyai.com/) offers unified evaluation across both. The [library](https://github.com/evidentlyai/evidently) downloaded over 25 million times provides 100+ built-in metrics covering data drift and feature quality for ML models, plus output quality and hallucination detection for LLMs.\n\nThe declarative testing API lets you define evaluation suites as code, running them in CI/CD or on production traffic. A visual interface helps explore results without writing queries. Evidently Cloud adds tracing, synthetic data generation, and alerting for teams wanting a managed experience.\n\nBest for teams running both traditional ML and LLM workloads who want one evaluation framework. The LLM-specific features are solid but not as deep as dedicated tools like TruLens or Galileo.\n\n### Our top pick\n\n**Arize Phoenix.** The OpenTelemetry foundation prevents vendor lock-in while the combination of tracing, evaluation, and prompt management covers most quality assurance needs. Open source with active development and a growing community.\n\n## Gateway and proxy\n\n_Best for: Cost optimization, routing, quick setup_\n\nGateway tools sit between your application and LLM providers, adding observability without changing your code. They're the fastest path to production monitoring, often requiring just a URL change or single header. The trade-off is depth: you get excellent cost tracking and request logging, but less sophisticated evaluation than dedicated platforms.\n\n### Helicone\n\n![Helicone LLM gateway dashboard with cost tracking, caching, and request logging](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/helicone.webp)\n\n[Helicone](https://www.helicone.ai/) wins on simplicity. Change your base URL or add a single header, and you're logging every LLM request in under two minutes. The [open source platform](https://github.com/Helicone/helicone) (4,800+ GitHub stars) supports 100+ models across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, and others through an OpenAI-compatible API.\n\nThe feature set focuses on practical production needs: unified billing across providers with zero markup, automatic fallbacks when a provider goes down, and response caching to cut costs on repeated queries. The dashboard surfaces costs, latency, and usage patterns without configuration.\n\nA generous free tier handles most development and early production needs. The limitation is evaluation depth. Helicone tracks what happened but doesn't assess whether responses were good. For quality measurement, you'll pair it with another tool.\n\n### Portkey\n\n![Portkey AI gateway supporting 250+ models with routing, fallbacks, and observability](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/portkey.webp)\n\n[Portkey](https://portkey.ai/) takes the gateway concept further with support for over 250 AI models and enterprise-grade infrastructure. The [open source gateway](https://github.com/Portkey-AI/gateway) (10,000+ GitHub stars) runs on global edge workers with just 20-40ms latency overhead, handling millions of requests per minute at 99.99% uptime.\n\nBeyond basic proxying, Portkey adds reliability features:\n\n- Automatic fallbacks between providers\n- Load balancing across model endpoints\n- Request caching and rate limiting\n- Semantic caching for similar queries\n\nThe platform is ISO 27001 and SOC 2 certified with GDPR and HIPAA compliance, making it viable for regulated industries. Enterprise customers can deploy in private cloud environments. The free tier covers 10,000 requests per month, with paid plans adding advanced routing and analytics.\n\nThe trade-off is complexity. Portkey does more than simple proxying, which means more configuration to get the most from it.\n\n### Traceloop (OpenLLMetry)\n\n![Traceloop OpenLLMetry open source LLM observability layer built on OpenTelemetry](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/traceloop.webp)\n\n[Traceloop](https://www.traceloop.com/) built [OpenLLMetry](https://github.com/traceloop/openllmetry) (6,600+ GitHub stars) as an open source observability layer based entirely on OpenTelemetry standards. If you already run Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana, New Relic, or another APM tool, OpenLLMetry pipes LLM traces directly into your existing stack.\n\nThe SDK approach requires adding initialization code rather than just changing URLs, but provides richer instrumentation. Decorators let you annotate workflows and tasks, creating detailed traces that show exactly how your application processes requests. SDKs cover Python, TypeScript, Go, and Ruby.\n\nTraceloop offers a managed platform for teams without existing observability infrastructure. The open source library is completely free, with the managed service adding dashboards and alerting. Best for teams who want LLM observability integrated with their existing monitoring rather than another separate tool.\n\n### Our top pick\n\n**Portkey.** The combination of 250+ model support, enterprise-grade reliability features, and active open source development makes it the most complete gateway option. Start with the free tier and scale up as needed.\n\n## Enterprise APM\n\n_Best for: Teams already on Datadog/New Relic_\n\nIf your organization already runs Datadog, New Relic, or Weights \u0026 Biases, adding LLM observability to your existing stack often makes more sense than introducing another tool. These platforms extend their core monitoring capabilities to cover AI workloads, keeping everything in one place.\n\n### Datadog LLM Observability\n\n![Datadog LLM Observability dashboard with AI tracing, cost monitoring, and security scanning](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/datadog.webp)\n\n[Datadog](https://www.datadoghq.com/) brings LLM monitoring into its unified observability platform. If you already use Datadog for APM, infrastructure, or logs, [LLM Observability](https://www.datadoghq.com/product/llm-observability/) adds AI-specific tracing without fragmenting your monitoring setup.\n\nThe SDK auto-instruments OpenAI, LangChain, AWS Bedrock, and Anthropic calls, capturing latency, token usage, and errors without code changes. Built-in evaluations detect hallucinations and failed responses, while security scanners flag [prompt injection](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/context-engineering) attempts and prevent data leaks. The 2025 release added LLM Experiments for testing prompt changes against production data before deployment.\n\nTraces appear alongside your existing APM data, letting you correlate LLM latency with database queries or infrastructure issues. Pricing follows Datadog's consumption model, which can get expensive at scale. The LLM features require an existing subscription and add incremental costs based on usage.\n\n### New Relic AI Monitoring\n\n![New Relic AI Monitoring with agentic AI visibility and service maps](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/new-relic.webp)\n\n[New Relic](https://newrelic.com/) extends its application performance platform to cover LLM workloads with [AI Monitoring](https://newrelic.com/platform/ai-monitoring), keeping AI observability inside the same stack you already use for backend services. The 2025 Agentic AI Monitoring release focuses specifically on multi-agent systems, providing visibility across interconnected agents and the services they depend on.\n\nKey differentiators:\n\n- Agents Service Map showing all interactions between AI agents\n- AI Trace View for step-by-step debugging with latency and error details\n- 50+ integrations covering LLMs, ML libraries, and [vector databases](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-vector-databases)\n- MCP Server integration for accessing observability data from GitHub Copilot, Claude, or Cursor\n\nNew Relic reports 30% quarter-over-quarter growth in AI Monitoring adoption, with Python and Node.js dominating customer deployments. Pricing follows the same consumption-based model as Datadog and requires an existing subscription. The platform excels at connecting AI issues to broader application performance problems.\n\n### W\u0026B Weave\n\n![Weights \u0026 Biases Weave LLM experiment tracking with tracing and cost monitoring](/images/blog/best-evals-frameworks/weave.webp)\n\nFor teams focused more on experimentation than production monitoring, [Weights \u0026 Biases](https://wandb.ai/) built [Weave](https://wandb.ai/site/weave) to extend their ML experiment tracking capabilities to LLM applications. Rather than competing with traditional APM tools, Weave brings the experiment-tracking approach you already know to AI workloads. The [open source library](https://github.com/wandb/weave) (1,000+ GitHub stars) adds tracing with a simple decorator: `@weave.op()` on any Python function.\n\nWeave automatically logs calls to OpenAI, Anthropic, and other LLM libraries, recording inputs, outputs, metadata, token usage, and costs. Traces appear in the familiar W\u0026B dashboard alongside your ML experiments, creating a unified view of model development and production behavior.\n\nThis works best for teams running both traditional ML models and LLM applications who want one platform for experiment tracking across both. Weave is free to start with included credits. The focus on MLOps workflows means teams needing alerting and incident response should look elsewhere.\n\n### Our top pick\n\n**Use what you have.** If you're already paying for Datadog, add LLM Observability. If you're on New Relic, use AI Monitoring. If you run W\u0026B for experiments, try Weave. The value here is consolidation, not features. Introducing a new vendor defeats the purpose of this category.\n\n## How to choose your tool\n\nWith 15 tools across four categories, the right choice depends on what you're building, how big your team is, and what tech stack you're using. Start by matching your application type to the tools designed for it.\n\n### By use case\n\n**[RAG applications](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-open-source-rag-frameworks)** need strong evaluation features. TruLens focuses on RAG metrics (context relevance, groundedness, answer relevance), while Phoenix offers OpenTelemetry integration for unified observability. End-to-end visibility in RAG systems means monitoring from data extraction (where tools like [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) structure web content) through retrieval and generation.\n\nIf you're building [**AI agents**](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/agentic-ai-trends) instead, you'll need detailed tracing for multi-step workflows. Langfuse and LangSmith work well for [debugging agent chains](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/build-documentation-agent-langgraph-firecrawl), showing you exactly where decisions happen. For teams already on Datadog or New Relic, their 2025 agentic monitoring features provide service maps across interconnected agents.\n\n**Chatbots** shift the focus to conversation quality and cost control. Helicone offers the fastest setup with automatic cost tracking. Galileo's Luna-2 models provide real-time guardrails if you need to block problematic responses before they reach users.\n\n### By team size\n\nYour team's size affects both what you need and what you can realistically maintain. **Solo developers or pairs** should start simple: Helicone gets you logging in minutes, while Langfuse offers more features with a generous free tier if you want full observability from day one.\n\n**Small teams (3-10 people)** benefit from all-in-one platforms that reduce maintenance overhead. Langfuse for its adaptability, LangSmith if you're already using LangChain.\n\nAs you scale to **mid-size teams (10-50)**, you can justify combining focused tools. Phoenix for evaluation plus Portkey for routing gives you depth without platform lock-in.\n\n**Enterprise teams (50+)** should extend existing infrastructure rather than adding another vendor. If you run Datadog or New Relic, add their LLM modules. Otherwise, deploy Langfuse self-hosted for data control.\n\n### By tech stack\n\nThe framework you're using often determines which tool integrates most smoothly. **[LangChain](/blog/best-open-source-agent-frameworks) users** get the most from LangSmith, where the integration is automatic and the debugging tools understand LangChain's internals.\n\n**[LlamaIndex](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-open-source-rag-frameworks) users** should look at Phoenix or Langfuse, both integrate well and provide good visibility into retrieval steps. For **vanilla API calls**, gateway tools like Helicone and Portkey work best since they require just a URL change or header to start logging.\n\n**Multi-framework setups** benefit from OpenTelemetry-based tools like Phoenix, TruLens, or OpenLLMetry that work across any stack. Teams running both **ML and LLM workloads** should consider Evidently AI or W\u0026B Weave for unified monitoring across both.\n\n**Still unsure?** Start with Langfuse if you want a full-featured platform, or Helicone if you need observability in production today. Both have generous free tiers and you can migrate later if your needs change.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nDon't overthink your first choice. Pick one tool from the category that matches your biggest pain point and start collecting data. You can always switch later.\n\nInstrument early, because adding observability after you're in production means you'll have no history when something breaks. Even a simple gateway like Helicone takes minutes to set up and gives you cost tracking from day one.\n\nStart with the basics: cost, latency, and error rates. Add quality metrics like hallucination detection once you have baseline visibility and know what actually matters for your application. Your observability stack will grow with your needs.\n\nOne thing observability can't fix is bad input data. For [RAG applications](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/modern-rag-tech-stack) especially, structured web data from tools like [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) prevents issues that would otherwise show up as mysterious quality drops in your dashboards.\n\nPick a tool, add instrumentation, and ship it.\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"9a:[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"container\",\"children\":[[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"border-b border-border-faint relative
